events.title: The New AI Coding Fabric - Patrick Debois, Tessl
events.description: AI-driven development is outgrowing the IDE and traditional build systems. A new layer of infrastructure is forming — the AI Coding Fabric — built to support autonomous coding agents..Exposing an MCP service is only the beginning. To truly enable AI-native software development, we’ll need systems for things like:- spec driven development versioning , spec evals and spec registries- support for long running coding sandboxes for our Agents- proxies that easily switch between different coding models and agents- review at scale of parallelized agent artefacts- coding guardrails and tracing/observability- knowledge base systems optimized for development learnings to improve ourselves , the team and the agentsThis is already taking shape through emerging tools and prototypes in the open-source and cloud-native ecosystems. You’ll see how these patterns might soon extend — and challenge — the foundations we’ve built around IDEs, CI/CD, and service-oriented architectures.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: How I Built My Laptop Into an MCP Server To Create Secure Cloud Native Infrastructure - Mahendran Selvakumar, Tata Consultancy Services Limited
events.description: Most teams still build new cloud native services by copying old YAML and hoping required labels, probes, limits, and security rules are correct. This is slow, hard to review, and does not scale across many clusters.In this session I show how I turned my laptop into an MCP server that can generate a first version of secure cloud native infrastructure from a simple request. I describe a service in plain language such as name, image, ports, rollout needs, access level, and autoscaling range. The server returns structured output plus a baseline security posture. This includes probes, required labels, resource requests and limits, safe container settings, and allowed traffic.MCP acts as the control layer. The server does not change clusters directly. It produces consistent and reviewable output that can be approved and rolled out in a predictable way, cutting review effort by about 40% and reducing drift across clusters by about 30%

events.description: While many organizations have seen initial success deploying LLM-integrated chatbots and agentic IDEs into internal or external production, the vast majority of agentic projects are still stuck in day 1 pilots with no clear path to production. This talk will share best practice patterns and architectures for agentic infrastructure in use across the agentgateway, kagent, and agentregistry communities to help you bring your agentic pilots to day 2 and beyond.

events.title: Agentics Day: MCP + Agents | Sponsored Keynote: Rescue Agents From Prototype Purgatory: Operationalize Agent Readiness - Ignasi Barrera, Tetrate.io
events.description: 2025 will be remembered as the year enterprises experimented with AI agents. By 2026, experimentation will no longer be enough. As agents begin influencing real workflows, decisions, and outcomes, organizations face a new challenge: defining when an agent is actually “ready” to operate. This session explores how Agent Readiness pairs qualitative standards with enforceable control points, enabling teams to rescue agents from infinite prototype loops while building and maintaining trust, consistency, and accountability. We'll explore how readiness reframes the software development lifecycle, and how changing how you work with AI is as important as what you decide to do with AI.

events.title: Beyond Vibecoding: The Coach/Player Model for Actual Autonomous Development - Douwe Osinga, Block
events.description: We introduce and demonstrate dialectical autocoding: a novel approach to AI-assisted software development that transcends the limitations of current vibe coding tools through a structured coach-player feedback loop. With a bounded, adversarial process between two cooperating agents, we show how AI can make substantially more progress on complex coding tasks, resulting in better tested, more robust outcomes, while working around fundamental attention and human-in-the-loop limitations.Both agents begin with the same goal: comprehensive requirements that describe the particulars of the problem. The requirements steer clear of implementation details or instructions, save certain basic guidance such as choice of programming language/target platform, allowing agents flexibility in arriving at its solution.We believe this approach to cooperative autocoding is able to maintain goal persistence (long-horizon tasks with zero interruptions to humans that are directing the work.

events.title: Keeping Your Agents in Check: Layered Security for Agentic Platforms in Production - Evaline Ju, IBM Research & Kelly Abuelsaad, IBM
events.description: As AI agents move into production, safety, reliability, and security must extend beyond the models themselves to every layer of the platform. This talk explores a layered approach to securing agentic platforms, from identity and authorization to guardrails and operational checks. We discuss how risks like prompt injection, manipulated outputs, and unauthorized tool access can be mitigated using both rule-based and AI-driven approaches. We will present a reference architecture where guardrails are applied at the Envoy gateway layer and cryptographically verifiable trust is established between agents and tools through OAuth 2.0 delegation with Keycloak.Attendees will learn the unique risks that accompany agentic applications along with practical strategies for scaling these safety mechanisms — all while maintaining performance and flexibility in production environments.

events.title: Fantastic Vibes and Where To Find Them - Erica Hughberg, Tetrate.io & Mauricio Salatino, Diagrid
events.description: Everyone's heard "I vibe-coded an entire architecture" or seen "AI built it beautifully but it doesn't work." This talk examines what actually works when teams use AI agents to code.We look at four categories: which pull requests ship versus get reverted, which agent bots developers love versus uninstall, whether AI helps non-developers contribute, and patterns separating productivity wins from frustration.Is isn't all about capability—it's also guidance. When teams use machine-readable conventions telling agents how projects work, productivity jumps. When they don't, agents fail silently. We'll review real cases of good and bad vibes, including one team safely removing 100K+ lines while another wasted days on broken code.You'll learn concrete patterns: narrow scope wins, validation gates catch issues, human review before merge, and hybrid workflows for different task types. Practical, evidence-based guidance on when AI amplifies productivity and when guardrails are essential.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Securing AI Agents: Applying Service Mesh Patterns To MCP - Shriya Chaitanya Kamat Tarcar, Nuvama Wealth Management Limited
events.description: Although most MCP implementations lack basic authentication, AI agents are being deployed with system-level permissions, database access and API keys. Microservices security crisis is being recreated by the cloud native community but with agents capable of code modification, data exfiltration and attack propagation.Informed by AI agent PoCs,this talk will show how service mesh security patterns translate directly to MCP deployments.To demonstrate how mutual TLS authentication, zero-trust boundaries and policy enforcement relate to inter-agent and agent-to-server communication, this talk will look at MCP protocol specification, Istio and Linkerd security models.Attendees will receive:1)Threat model that connects MCP attack vectors with locations in service mesh.2)Authentication patterns for MCP servers. 3)Framework for deploying security patterns.Before production deployments cause supply chain incidents, session will provide the security foundation that agentic ecosystem needs.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: How AI Agents Are Ending Interrupt-Driven Platform Work - Sunny Whang, Cisco
events.description: Platform engineers face relentless interrupts - supporting dozens of service owners while drowning in repetitive tickets for upgrades, provisioning, and troubleshooting. We built a multi-agent system at Outshift by Cisco to solve this internally. After proving its impact, we open-sourced it as CAIPE: Community AI Platform Engineering (now part of CNOE) - because this pain point affects platform teams everywhere.This talk shows how we achieved our deep supervisor agent that orchestrates complex tasks by coordinating sub-agents in parallel and sequential workflows. We use GraphRAG for internal platform knowledge and our OpenAPI MCP tool to rapidly integrate platform tools. Agents handle tasks from simple tickets to complex operations across ArgoCD, Backstage, Jira, PagerDuty, AWS, and more.You'll see our production demo, architecture breakdown, and practical lessons from deploying agents in production.

events.title: ArgoCon l Sponsored Keynote: From Kubernetes to Anything: The Evolution of Promotion with Kargo - Jesse Suen, Akuity
events.description: Kargo began as a Kubernetes-native promotion tool, designed to safely and continuously move container images and manifests through environments using GitOps principles. As delivery pipelines evolved, so did Kargo, expanding beyond clusters to coordinate the promotion of Terraform plans, cloud infrastructure, and other non-Kubernetes changes alongside application workloads.Today, Kargo is taking the next step. With user-defined container steps, promotion workflows are no longer limited to predefined integrations. Teams can now run arbitrary, versioned logic as part of a promotion, whether that’s executing custom validations, invoking external APIs, mutating infrastructure, running tests, or integrating with systems that don’t yet have first-class support.In this talk, we’ll show how Kargo has evolved from a Kubernetes-focused tool into a universal promotion layer. By combining GitOps, policy-driven workflows, and user-defined containers, Kargo enables safe, auditable promotion across Kubernetes, Terraform, and virtually any system your delivery pipeline depends on, all within a single declarative workflow.

events.title: Bring Your Own Artifact Driver To Workflows - Alan Clucas, Pipekit
events.description: Argo Workflows 4.0 introduces artifact plugins - a powerful extensibility mechanism that allows you to integrate any storage backend or implement a custom artifact driver without modifying Argo's core. Previously, adding support for a new storage system required contributing code to Argo itself. Now, plugins enable organizations to integrate unsupported or proprietary storage systems. Plugins are language-agnostic, containerized GRPC servers that run alongside workflow pods as sidecars and init containers.In this talk we'll walk through creating a plugin. This involves implementing a GRPC server as a docker image.The plugin feature introduces pod management complexities: plugins must be available to the argo-server, and during the init and runtime phases of pods that require them. I'll go over some of these complexities and how they were solved, giving attendees insight into how argo-workflows pods and artifacts are orchestrated.

events.title: GitOps and Secrets: State of the Union - Kostis Kapelonis, Octopus Deploy
events.description: One of the most popular questions for people adopting GitOps is what to do with secrets. Argo CD version 3.x finally has a clear recommendation on how to handle secrets with Kubernetes. More specifically, the new proposal is to externalize secret management entirely instead of using plugins or other similar mechanisms to inject secrets during application deployment.In this talk we will reevaluate and compare all the popular secret solutions (sealed-secrets, sops, vault-plugin, external-secret-operator, csi-drivers, etc.) and explain where they differ and the pros/cons of each.We will also explain the common pitfalls of using secrets with Argo CD and discuss the best practices for good secret hygiene, such as secret rotation and storage.

events.title: Anatomy of a GitOps Engine: Understanding Your Sync - Alexandre Gaudreault, Intuit
events.description: Everyone clicks "sync", and just expects it to work. But what really happens under the hood? The Argo CD sync process is a powerful and complex dance, and its heart - the GitOps Engine - has evolved.This session dives deep into the anatomy of the engine responsible for making your desired state a reality. We will follow a single Application sync step-by-step, from the initial sync operation request to the final "Synced" status. We'll demystify how different sync options, resource hooks, delete policies, sync waves, retries and more interact to build a precise execution plan. You'll see exactly how the engine orchestrates complex, multi-stage deployments and how you can leverage these features to manage dependencies and control rollout order.If you've ever wondered why a resource didn't sync the way you expected it to sync, this talk is for you.

events.description: As BYD’s autonomous driving business scales rapidly, our platform processes tens of millions of data records daily, requiring efficient pipelines for cleaning, labeling, and repairing.We migrated from Airflow to Kubernetes‑native Argo Workflows and built a system that manages 3,000+ GPUs and runs over 1M data tasks per day.We will share challenges and solutions from building the platform, including:• GPU Utilization – Optimizing thousand‑GPU clusters for higher utilization and enabling multi‑tenant resource sharing.• High Concurrency – Sustaining millions of tasks with advanced concurrency control to boost throughput.• Extreme‑Scale Stability – Resolving scheduling challenges at 300k+ workflow scale, such as informer update delays and controller memory spikes.• Community Contribution – Upstreaming fixes and scalability improvements to the Argo Workflows project.Attendees will gain strategies to orchestrate massive GPU‑intensive workflows and ensure stability at scale.

events.title: Beyond Argo Rollouts: Boosting Developer Experience With Intelligent AIOps - Carlos Sanchez, Adobe & Kevin Dubois, IBM
events.description: Software rollouts can be painful and complex. Tools like Argo Rollouts provide a robust foundation to ease the pain, but with Cloud Native AI we can revolutionize the developer experience around it. This session explores elevating your release process by:* Integrating Agentic AI: Analyze rollout failures using AI agents.* Leveraging Models Deployed on Kubernetes: Utilize scalable AI models that are in your control.* Employing Intelligent Coding Agents: These cloud-based agents can make code corrections based on the failure analysis and create automated PRs, and timely notifications.* Embracing Human-in-the-Loop Oversight: Provide developers with confidence and control by allowing them to review and course correct if needed.This strategic combination of Cloud Native tools leads to significantly reduced Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR) and a more resilient, productive developer experience. Join us to discover the future of seamless software delivery.

events.title: Don't Do What Donny Don't Does: The 10 Dos and 500 Don’ts of Workflows Success - Tim Collins, Pipekit & Becky Pauley, Tailscale
events.description: Think your Argo Workflows cluster is production-ready just because it runs? Think again! While getting up and running takes no time at all, the real operational complexity happens when Day 2 Operations kick in. That’s when subtle misconfigurations, scaling challenges, and security gaps emerge, threatening reliability and uptime. Day 2 Operations mean grappling with real-world issues: debugging Workflow failures, enforcing security best practices, and avoiding performance bottlenecks.Join us for a deep dive into the top pitfalls observed in production. Drawing from real customer incidents, we’ll unpack the most frequent missteps and their consequences.Whether you're running a handful of Workflows or planning to scale to enterprise volumes, this talk will transform your understanding of what it takes to run Argo Workflows reliably. Expect actionable guidance, hard-won lessons, and field-proven solutions you can put to work immediately. No theory. Just real ops, real outcomes.

events.title: Panel: Building a GitOps Culture - Dan Garfield & Revital Barletz, Octopus Deploy; Antonela Cukurin, Novo Nordisk & Gabriel Quennesson, Michelin
events.description: We spend 90% of our time talking about GitOps from a technical perspective but that is not where organizations struggle. Instead, they stall on building a GitOps Culture. In many ways, a GitOps Culture is a realization of DevOps culture. Enabling self-service, independence of action and ownership, and clean audit trails are part of GitOps culture.Argo CD and Argo Rollouts are largely unopinionated about culture, but most implementation decisions are downstream of culture. In this panel, we’ll hear from end users and experts alike about how to build a culture that enables GitOps and some of the technical hallmarks that show you’re on the right path.

events.title: Panel: From Signals To Safety Nets: Self-Healing Progressive Delivery With Argo Rollouts - Anastasiia Gubska, Chase UK; Julia Furst Morgado, Dash0; Joe Fuller, Buoyant
events.description: What if your deployment pipeline could sense failure and heal itself before users notice?In this session, we show how to build self-healing progressive delivery pipelines using Argo Rollouts powered by real-time observability signals. Integrating Prometheus, OpenTelemetry, and Linkerd, we transform rollouts from time-based experiments into signal-driven systems that auto-react to performance degradation.See how:- A canary detects rising latency and auto-rolls back early before wider exposure- Error spikes trigger KEDA-based scaling and pause Rollouts until stable- Service-mesh insights feed AnalysisTemplates for closed-loop remediationWhile we use specific tools, the patterns are universal: any mesh, any metrics stack, any automation framework. You’ll take home templates, blueprints, and decision frameworks that make Argo Rollouts the control plane for resilience, ushering in the next phase of progressive delivery: Automated Resilience Engineering.

events.title: Analysis in Action: Real-World Metrics Driven Argo Rollouts With AnalysisTemplates - Chris Detsicas & Antonio Jimenez Martinez, Cisco ThousandEyes
events.description: Rolling out changes in Kubernetes safely goes beyond canaries and blue-green deployments - it requires automated health checks. Argo Rollouts’ AnalysisTemplates let us bake automated checks and business logic into every rollout. Checks include success rate SLOs with Prometheus, business-hour release gates, API health checks and even dynamic E2E tests. These tests help us build confidence in our releases and catch issues early while only promoting what’s proven healthy. In this talk, we’ll share how we have implemented AnalysisTemplates and walk through real examples using Prometheus metrics.

events.title: GitOps Your Costs: Automated FinOps Through Argo Workflows - Kunal Das, Cast AI & Hrittik Roy, vCluster
events.description: GitOps revolutionized deployment practices now it's time to apply those same principles to cost optimization. This session demonstrates how to build automated FinOps pipelines using Argo Workflows and Events.From our past experience cost optimization workflows that automatically rightsize deployments based on utilization metrics, implement progressive cost reduction through Argo Rollouts, and trigger cleanup workflows for idle resources. We'll show real implementations where teams reduced waste by 45% through GitOps-driven optimization. Discover patterns for cost-aware CI/CD that prevents expensive misconfigurations from reaching production, automated weekend scale-downs for development environments, and policy-as-code for resource limits. events.title: [ROOM UPDATED] Argo CD: Previewing Pull Request Changes in SECONDS! - Dag Bjerre Andersen, Egmont & Sergey Shevchenko, TangoMe
events.description: As organizations increasingly adopt GitOps, accurately visualizing manifest changes before they merge has become crucial.At ArgoCon 2024 we presented a method that used ephemeral clusters to render precise diffs on pull requests - it was accurate but slow. This talk introduces a new approach that keeps the accuracy but reduces the wait time from minutes to seconds! Instead of spinning up a new cluster for every PR, we connect each preview to a pre-configured Argo CD instance on standby.The talk will showcase how this is used at Egmont to visualize manifest changes across repositories with hundreds of applications in just seconds, and how developers at Tango integrate this technique into their CI/CD pipeline to preview changes to 1,900 applications across 25+ clusters.Through real-world examples, Argo CD admins will learn how to set up accurate and fast diff previews on their pull requests, resulting in faster code reviews, shorter feedback loops, and fewer failed deployments.

events.title: [ROOM UPDATED] From Canary To Global: Unified Progressive Delivery for Hybrid Cloud With Karmada & Argo - Zhuang Zhang, Huawei Technologies Co. Ltd & Ryan Wu, Netease Cloud Music
events.description: As enterprises adopt hybrid cloud and multi-cluster architectures for HA, compliance, and edge deployment, application delivery grows complex. Manual cross-cluster progressive delivery is inefficient and risky. Most progressive delivery tools are single-cluster, lacking global coordination.We demonstrate how Karmada integrates with Argo CD and Argo Rollouts to build a unified hybrid-cloud progressive delivery platform—enabling "define once, deliver safely anywhere": a single declarative spec drives consistent, automated rollouts across all clusters. Now, the platform is proven in production.In this sharing, we’ll go over:1. Key challenges and difficulties of progressive delivery in multi-cloud scenarios;2. Problems and solutions in deploying and integrating Karmada and Argo, as well as the amazing things they can achieve together;3. Configuration details and overall workflow of Karmada and Argo in advanced deployment capabilities, demonstrated through a canary release example.

events.title: Decoupling Canary Deployments From DBs With Argo Rollouts - Anastasiia Gubska, Chase UK
events.description: Argo Rollouts offers well-known strategies for stateless applications, but what about deploying stateful applications that use databases? How can teams ensure safe, consistent, and reversible database updates when rolling out new application versions? This talk answers by showing how to decouple database migrations from application deployments through backward compatible schema changes to the database that allow both old and new application versions to operate simultaneously during the rollouts. We’ll also explore practical strategies for common database refactoring scenarios, including adding new columns, renaming columns and tables, and introducing new data types gracefully.

events.title: Network Segmentation at Scale: GitOps-Driven Multi-Tenant Isolation With Argo CD - Luke Philips & Christian Hernandez, Cisco
events.description: Argo CD is built from the ground up with multitenancy in mind, providing administrators with powerful tools needed to support many teams. However, how does multitenancy work/look like when implemented? Production grade? This talk explores the complete picture: we will take you through all the considerations in building an isolated platform for multitenancy.We will take a look on how to GitOps hundreds of tenants without creating network policy nightmare. Architectural patterns for tenant isolation using Cilium Network Policies managed through ArgoCD. ArgoCD Network Concerns and Best Practices. Tenancy with Projects and RBAC strategies for tenant self-service and compliance audit trails. Learn best practices, common pitfalls, and tested patterns from our real world experience running multi-tenant systems in production.

events.title: Beyond GitOps: Building Intelligent Drift Detection and Auto-Remediation in Argo CD - Ram Mohan Rao Chukka, JFrog & Shibi Ramachandran, ING
events.description: In most of the Kubernetes clusters, configuration drift is unavoidable and is often hard to detect until something goes wrong. ArgoCD does a great job with basic drift detection, self-healing and auto-sync, but in real-world enterprise environments, not all drift is equal. Scaling a deployment isn’t the same as deleting a secret, and treating them the same can lead to trouble.In this talk, we’ll walk through how to extend ArgoCD to handle drift more intelligently. Using Custom Health Checks, ApplicationSets, Resource Hooks, and a custom controller, we built a system that understands the severity of changes and responds accordingly by auto-remediating safe changes, triggering approvals for sensitive updates, and rolling back when things go wrong.This talk is ideal for platform engineers, SREs, and DevOps teams looking to evolve their GitOps strategy with smarter automation, policy enforcement, and enterprise-grade reliability.

events.title: Cross-Cluster Progressive Delivery: Rolling Out Apps With Argo Rollouts, Workflows and Events - Carlos Santana & Elamaran Shanmugam, Amazon Web Services
events.description: You've mastered canary deployments within a cluster. But rolling out apps across 50 clusters spanning regions and clouds? Most organizations deploy everywhere and pray, or spend weeks on manual rollouts.We treat multi-cluster rollouts like canary deployments at the cluster level. Start with dev clusters, validate success, progress to staging, then production region by region—with automated validation and rollback.We combine Argo Workflows and Argo Events to orchestrate cluster progression and Argo Rollouts for deployments. Workflows define which cluster groups, in what order, with what validation. Rollouts execute progressive delivery within each cluster while Workflows control cross-cluster blast radius. You'll see: live demo rolling an app across tiers; automated validation before progression; handling failures; integrating ArgoCD ApplicationSets.

events.title: Agnostic Workload Identity Across the Argo Ecosystem Using SPIFFE and SPIRE - Andrew Block, Red Hat & Blake Pettersson, Akuity
events.description: In modern cloud-native environments, securing workload-to-workload communication is critical. This session explores implementing platform-agnostic workload identity across the Argo ecosystem from Argo CD, Argo Workflows, Argo Rollouts, and Argo Events using SPIFFE (Secure Production Identity Framework For Everyone) and SPIRE. Learn how the SPIFFE project aids in the elimination of static credentials and implements zero-trust security by providing each workload with a cryptographically verifiable identity. We’ll demonstrate practical patterns for integrating SPIRE across multiple Argo projects, enabling secure service-to-service authentication, automated certificate rotation, and fine-grained authorization policies. Discover how this approach scales across multi-cluster and hybrid cloud environments while remaining vendor-neutral. Gain firsthand experience with real-world examples of securing GitOps workflows, CI/CD pipelines, and progressive delivery strategies that you can use today.

events.title: From FTP To Argo CD & Argo Rollouts: An Adoption History Inside a Corp - Alfonso Ming, LIDL HUG DIGITAL & Meritxell Rodríguez, SCRM Lidl International Hub
events.description: One year ago, we deployed our SSO using Azure App Services and Azure Pipelines. Now we use semi-automatic releases driven by ArgoCD and Argo Rollouts. During this session, we will explore how we have managed this migration process without downtime in a critical mission software with more than 90 million active users but also explaining why we have chosen these tools and what problems we've faced with. We will also show how our teams interacts with Argo tools, having in mind that our teams are owners of their products but not of ArgoCD instance. This includes things such as the RBAC, the management of secrets or clusters/repos and also how we have taken advantage of appsets and cluster generators as part of our Disaster Recovery plan. The last but not least, we will explain how we can guarantee the reliability of the systems thanks to execute "last mile" smoke test in the beginning of a canary rollout (managed by Argo rollouts).

events.title: [ROOM UPDATED] Declarative...ish? Fixing Hidden Argo CD Pitfalls in Your GitOps Setup - Regina Voloshin, Octopus Deploy
events.description: In the rush to adopt Argo CD and implement GitOps‑driven delivery, many teams start strong but then quietly accumulate structural and architectural mistakes. These missteps may seem innocuous at first, but over time they erode developer productivity, reduce clarity, introduce fragile deployments and undermine the very benefits of GitOps. This talk will zero in on four key anti‑patterns:1. Creating Argo CD Applications in a dynamic way2. Writing Applications instead of Application Sets3. Using Helm to package Applications instead of Application Sets4. Hardcoding Helm data inside Argo CD ApplicationsTogether we will examine the hidden costs of those patterns, how they manifest in teams at scale, and concrete strategies to avoid them. Attendees will walk away with actionable patterns to ensure their Argo CD + GitOps architecture remains maintainable, scalable and developer‑friendly.

events.title: [ROOM UPDATED] Leveraging Argo Workflows To Move, Migrate and Manage All of Your Workflows - John Keates, Wehkamp Retail Group

By constructing composable pipelines with workflows we also got a lot of re-use out of common steps like linting, security checks and notifications.In this presentation, we'll highlight how we arrived at these projects and what went in to designing this system.

events.description: When your Argo CD setup grows to hundreds of applications, things can go wrong in surprising ways. When you scale Argo CD to hundreds of applications (500+ and beyond), the Repo Server often becomes the biggest performance bottleneck.It constantly polls Git repositories and regenerates manifests even when nothing has changed.This leads to:• High CPU and memory usage• Slower syncs and reconciliations• Delayed deployments across teamsIn this session, we’ll share how we fixed it. events.title: [ROOM UPDATED] ⚡Lightning Talk: “Tilt? Goreman? Make? Go Run? A Contributor’s Guide To Argo CD Local Dev” - Nitish Kumar, Akuity
events.description: A common question in the Argo CD Slack by new contributors is “How do I run and debug Argo CD locally?”. As a distributed system made up of several loosely-coupled components, Argo CD can feel overwhelming to new contributors who just want to test a change or debug an issue. Should you use Make targets, run components individually with go run, use goreman or use Tilt?In this talk, we’ll break down the different ways to run and debug Argo CD locally depending on what part of the project you’re working on, when to use tools like go run, make, goreman, and Tilt with a live demo. You’ll learn how to run just the components you need to debug an issue, and how to configure your favorite. IDE to debug components smoothly.Whether you're fixing a bug in argocd-server, adding logic to the controller, trying to understand why an Argo CD component isn't picking up changes, or have ever asked “which component handles this?”, this session will get you productive faster as an Argo CD contributor.

events.title: Our Journey To a Unified Developer Experience With Backstage - Symbat Nurbay & Xicu Piñera, Booking.com
events.description: Over the past 3 years, our organization has used Backstage as a central portal where 1.5k employees access 45 custom tools and 400K software assets. But to most people, it felt like many separate tools living side-by-side—helpful but not unified—and we realized we weren’t using Backstage's full potential. Last year, we rethought our approach. Instead of treating the portal as a container for tools, we aimed to create a unified experience that helps people work across them. The idea came from our weekly meetings, where we constantly switched between tools to check health, metrics, and alerts for our team's services. That workflow was slow and fragmented. To fix it, we built a centralized place that connects key signals from those tools in one view. This has changed how we run weekly reviews and how we use the portal daily. This talk shares our path from “a collection of tools” to a unified experience—how we redesigned it, the technical and organizational challenges, and what we learned.

events.description: How long does it take to go from zero Backstage knowledge to shipping a plugin? This talk shares my experience building a “team mood” plugin during a six-week internship, with no prior TypeScript, React, or Backstage experience.My background in the arts and humanities, and now in media engineering, shaped how I approached this challenge, focusing on human experience rather than infrastructure. The plugin was completed, deployed, and used internally. I’ll show how I learned the stack fundamentals, explored Backstage’s architecture, and set up a working build and deployment pipeline within that timeframe. I’ll walk through concrete timelines: which concepts took days versus hours, the resources that accelerated progress, and the roadblocks that slowed me down.For teams evaluating Backstage adoption or planning developer onboarding, this journey offers a practical reference point with real data, key learnings, and a reminder that creativity and empathy are just as valuable as code.

events.title: BackstageCon | Sponsored Keynote - Your Backstage, Our Grunt Work: Migrating to Spotify Portal Without Pausing Your Roadmap - Joe Combopiano, Spotify
events.description: You adopted Backstage to accelerate your developers, but somewhere along the way your platform team became full-time infrastructure maintainers — wrangling upgrades, patching security issues, and absorbing breaking changes. Spotify Portal is the SaaS, Spotify-managed edition of Backstage, built by the same engineers who created and open-sourced it. In this talk, we'll walk through what a real migration looks like, who owns what, and how your team keeps full control of plugins, data, and integrations without carrying the operational burden. We'll also preview the capabilities Portal unlocks once you're migrated — from AI-powered developer tools to scorecards, feature flags, and executive-level DevEx insights — the same tools Spotify uses internally, now available to you. You'll leave with a clear picture of how to stop keeping the lights on and start building what's next.

events.title: BackstageCon | Sponsored Keynote - Building an IDP in 5 Minutes Not 5 Months - Matar Peles, Port
events.description: Building an internal developer portal used to take months. Weeks of scoping, sprints of plugin development, endless YAML configurations, and a dedicated team just to get a service catalog live. By the time it shipped, half the requirements had changed.What if you could do it in minutes?In this session, I’ll build a fully functional developer portal live on stage - from an empty screen to a working platform - using nothing but natural language prompts. A service catalog mapped to real infrastructure, self-service actions with manual approvals, dashboards tracking engineering standards, and custom logic tailored to how your organization works.But that’s just the foundation for what comes next.Developer portals are evolving from dashboards engineers look at into platforms that act on their behalf. Once your portal understands your services, ownership model, and standards, it has the context to power agentic workflows that go beyond a UI. I’ll show that progression live: from catalog, to self-service, to an agentic workflow that triages and resolves an incident on its own.Whether you’re running Backstage, evaluating alternatives, or starting from scratch - you’ll walk away with a concrete picture of where developer portals are headed, how fast you can get there, and what “agentic” actually looks like when it’s not just a buzzword.

events.title: Divide & Collaborate: Creating Scalable and Healthy Backstage Based Applications in a Big Organisation - Krzysztof Janota & Dusan Askovic, ING Bank N.V.
events.description: Backstage is a tool with a lot of potential and almost limitless possibilities. It works great when you are starting from scratch, without any legacy, or in smaller sized organisations. Building innovative new features with just a few people is easy. But how do you scale that approach? How do you make innovation is community driven? How do you make sure Backstage is still doing what you envisioned without polluting it with unwanted features? How do you make sure you’re not cutting down innovations?Large scale enterprises often find themselves with dozens of developers with great ideas but no way of contributing to the success that Backstage can bring to their organisation.This talk will describe tried and tested best practices and how we approach Backstage development as community in ING on a global scale.

events.title: Agentic Backstage: How To Manage an AI Software Catalog - Sam Nixon, Roadie
events.description: AI and Backstage should be great friends, but whether you’re working in a AI engineering team responsible for which models and architectures should be in use, an application engineer just trying to connect to the the models and MCP servers you need for development, or a Platform team trying to keep it all coherent and secure, this information can be hard to access. When you layer on top autonomous agents making their own actions, trying to maintain a useful Backstage instance in an AI world can be maddening.In this talk, we will share our experiences developing agents for Backstage, modelling AI assets in the Catalog, and what we think the role of Backstage will be as agents and AI tools become more prevalent. We’ll discuss how Backstage should be integral for AI-driven development and what we could change as a community to make sure of that future. Finally, we’ll walk you through how to set up your own AI Catalog and how it can be extended both inside and outside the portal.

events.title: Panel: Building a Healthy Backstage Plugins Ecosystem - Paul Schultz & Hope Hadfield, Red Hat; Heikki Hellgren, OP Financial Group; Peter Macdonald, VodafoneZiggo; Aramis Sennyey, DoorDash
events.description: Backstage’s plugin ecosystem is a key draw for the Backstage product, enabling teams to extend the platform to meet their own needs. But as the ecosystem matures across both the core and Community Plugins repository, new challenges emerge around sustainability, quality, and governance. This panel brings together maintainers and contributors from across the Backstage community to explore what it means to build and maintain healthy plugins in this evolving landscape. We’ll dig into questions like:What’s the biggest barrier for a new contributor, and how can we make that experience smoother?How can we balance innovation and its adoption (i.e. the new frontend system) with the need for quality and efficient plugin maintenance?What lessons can we draw from how different repositories handle ownership, testing, and dependency management, and where should those practices converge?This discussion will map out where Backstage’s plugin ecosystem stands today, and where it needs to go next.

events.title: Forget Rebuilding, Install Plugins at Runtime - David Festal & Jon Koops, Red Hat
events.description: As your Backstage instance scales, the 'rebuild and redeploy' cycle for every plugin update becomes a critical bottleneck, slowing down development and consuming resources. We'll show you how to transform your deployment from a monolithic build into a modular platform, dramatically simplifying the integration cycle, unlocking powerful cloud deployment options, and enabling true distributed ownership.

events.title: From the Ranch(er) to the Dev: A Case Study About Simplifying K8s Cluster Setup With Backstage - Alex Speranza, ImolaInformatica SPA
events.description: The two main features of a developer portal are the catalog of the resources and the capability of executing actions on those resources. Most of these actions can be reduced to two categories: managing the state of resources or running one-shot tasks with them as parameters. While in the former case a GitOps approach can be adopted, making the update of the resource state a matter of editing the content of a git repository, the latter has more obstacles, such as its audit or its possible non-idempotence.A challenging task is the onboarding of a cluster, because its link with an SCM must be performed as a one-shot task, but its configuration should follow a GitOps approach (for its idempotency).This talk follows the exploration of a cluster onboarding process involving Rancher, Backstage, and Gitlab, while also providing a real-world example of how to facilitate deployments on cluster where security practices, like the use of Sealed Secret, make the developers' job complicated.

events.title: Network Flow Aggregation: Pay for the Logs You Care About! - Mereta Degutyte, Anubhab Majumdar & Vamsi Kalapala, Microsoft
events.description: Hubble flow logs are a lifesaver for debugging and security. Until you look at the cloud storage bill.Millions of near-identical flows pile up, turning observability into an expensive 'nice-to-have' when it should be a 'must-have'!We’ve had ways to trim logs before by using FieldMask and Include/Exclude filters or using set times to make flows more relevant and less costly, but those only go so far.Our new approach - Flow Aggregation. Instead of storing each flow, we aggregate in-agent based on fields user actually cares about.The result? Lower storage overhead, quicker troubleshooting, and leaner data that’s ideal for analytics and machine learning.And yes, Calico users have had this for a while, now Cilium does too.In this talk, we’ll walk through this new feature and why it matters, show a live demo comparing raw and aggregated flows, and share practical tips for tuning aggregation to your workloads.If you love observability but hate runaway costs, this session is for you.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Accelerating Cilium: Our Journey on Offloading Cilium To DPU - Vijay Inavolu, Marvell Technologies
events.description: Can we offload Cilium 'completely' to a DPU ? How about doing this for a primary network ? Can it be done without adding any complexity at user level ? What if all nodes in the cluster don't have a DPU, can it still work ? While we are at it, can we also use the DPU as a node ? In a solution like this, how much of host resources can be saved ? What if security polices are present which are HW accelerated, how do these numbers look like ? Can we keep Cilium code changes minimal ? And can such a solution be opensource and DPU vendor neutral ?These are some of the questions that we have started with for our work on offloading Cilium to DPU. In this session we would like to walk you through the challenges that we have encountered and the solutions we have created to achieve our goal. Demonstrated with Marvell Octeon 10 DPU we will show how this solution transparently offloads Cilium. We also have some intriguing metrics that can be helpful for end users of Cilium

events.title: Cloud Native Promises, On-Premises: Bump Your Load Balancing To V2 With Cilium - Mikael Johansson Länsberg, Etraveli Group AB
events.description: Etraveli Group manages millions of daily flight searches in our on-premises data centers. Our legacy load balancing relied on proprietary hardware appliances, which often failed to deliver the agility, scalability and dynamic environment demanded by modern applications. This made keeping our Cloud-Native Promises, which rely on those very attributes, very difficult.We solved this by leveraging Cilium to bump our ingress and load balancing architecture one major version, to a v2:* Unified Control Plane: Integrating LB-IPAM, BGP, and Gateway API to replace the proprietary stack.* Performance: Powered by eBPF to handle millions of requests with low latency and high throughput.* True Cloud-Native: Seamlessly integrates with Kubernetes, enabling dynamic scaling, multi-tenancy and resilience.Join us to learn how we designed and deployed the next major version of load balancing, and how you could do it on your own infrastructure with Cilium!

events.description: Like the canals of Amsterdam, scaling Kubernetes security requires careful engineering, balancing the flow of policies and enforcement without flooding the system with overhead. Each policy acts like a gate in a waterway, controlling what passes through but too many and performance can quickly overflow. Enforcing security policies for every single workload introduces measurable performance and memory overhead. This talk follows SUSE's exploration of approaches to achieve strong enforcement while keeping overhead under control with Tetragon:- Designing and implementing a per-workload security model- Performance challenges observed- Techniques to reduce overheadAttendees will gain insights into eBPF policy design, performance optimization, and Kubernetes security scaling that flows as smoothly as Amsterdam’s waterways.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Closing the Gap: Fair North–South Bandwidth Management for Tenants - Weizhou Lan, Daocloud
events.description: In multi-tenant Kubernetes clusters, enforcing unified north–south ingress bandwidth limits is essential to ensure fair resource sharing. Although this sounds straightforward, Kubernetes currently lacks an effective solution.Most CNI plugins only support per-Pod ingress bandwidth control. As workloads scale out with multiple replicas, it becomes impossible to enforce consistent limits at the tenant or application level. On the other hand, community LoadBalancer implementations do not provide ingress bandwidth management for LoadBalancer Services. As a result, operators often have to rely on external components outside the Kubernetes.In this talk, we will present a lightweight, in-cluster approach built on Cilium CNI combined with Spiderpool. This design enables native bandwidth limiting for LoadBalancer Services, making it possible to achieve tenant-level ingress bandwidth management within Kubernetes, without adding external complexity.

events.title: Federated llm-d: Elevating Distributed Inference Beyond Cluster Boundaries - Madhuri Yechuri, Elotl & Abhishek Malvankar, IBM
events.description: .As generative AI models trend towards becoming commodity, the ability to serve them in a cost-effective high-performant fashion across hardware accelerators impacts time to market for Enterprise AI Initiatives. Llm-d is a popular open-source AI Framework that meets this exact need.Models are sensitive to hardware accelerators. Hardware accelerators are expensive and in short supply. Model serving time cannot and should not be impacted by unavailability of desired accelerator in a given Kubernetes cluster. The desired accelerator might just be a hop away in a different cluster in same/different region in same/different cloud. It would be prudent to source accelerators from all available clusters to deliver state-of-the-art performance to meet Enterprise SLAs.This talk presents a federated llm-d framework which stretches llm-d across multiple clusters. We will discuss use cases for federated distributed inference, and present a blueprint for the federated stack along with a demo.

events.title: Cloud Native AI + Kubeflow Day | Sponsored Keynote: Building Your AI Factory: Where Data Science and Platform Engineering Meet - Andreea Munteanu, Spectro Cloud
events.description: AI adoption at scale requires more than great models. It forces enterprises to rethink how their operational practices support specialized hardware, integrated tooling across the ML lifecycle, and enhanced security measures to protect sensitive data in an automated and reliable manner. Platform engineers and data scientists naturally focus on different areas. There is a natural skill gap between platform maintainers and end users, creating risks such as underutilized resources, overly complex architectures, and fragile day 2 operations. Layered on top are stringent security and compliance requirements for every component of the solution.In this session, we explore how cloud-native architectures and Kubernetes-based platforms provide the foundation for building AI factories that are scalable, modular, and fully automated. We’ll break down the essential components of an AI factory and explain how they can be easily consumed in a self-service manner across different use cases, such as model deployment. We will share lessons learned from real-world deployments, highlighting what works, what doesn’t, and how teams have adapted their architectures and processes along the way.

events.title: Cloud Native AI + Kubeflow Day | Sponsored Keynote: The 2026 Infrastructure, Operations, and Cloud Services Agenda: Scaling Agentic AI with Platform Engineering - Kevin Cochrane, Vultr
events.description: 2026 is the year heads of IOCS need to embrace AI, innovate, and move faster - all while cutting costs, scaling globally, and safeguarding the business against security and compliance risks.  For 2026, heads of IOCS need a simple, straightforward path to deliver core platform services to their software development and data scientist teams.  To deliver platform services, heads of IOCs need their platform engineering team to deliver the pre-tested development environments, models, and hardened infrastructure templates to connect data pipelines with GPU customers and scale up and scale down resources on-demand.  In this session, learn how Platform Engineers can take charge of accelerating enterprise AI infrastructure and transforming IOCS.

events.title: Cloud Native AI + Kubeflow Day | Sponsored Keynote: Production AI Runs on Open Source Abstract - Gleb Evstropov, Nebius
events.description: Behind today’s large-scale AI systems is a layered open-source ecosystem. Kubernetes provides the foundation, Slurm powers high-performance batch scheduling, Ray enables distributed compute, and frameworks like PyTorch and DeepSpeed drive model training. At Nebius, we build our AI platform directly on top of these technologies — integrating cloud-native orchestration with HPC scheduling to support large-scale training and inference workloads. We’ve also contributed back to the ecosystem by open-sourcing Soperator, a project that bridges Slurm and Kubernetes to combine topology-aware scheduling with cloud-native elasticity and automation.In this short talk, we’ll walk through how these open-source components fit together in real production environments and why building on open foundations enables better scalability, portability, and operational transparency for teams running AI at scale.

events.title: Ethical Bias - Robert Glenn, Glennium, LLC & Diana Zhawn, Kopacetic, Inc.
events.description: In this talk, we explore how to design ethical, behavior-aware AI agents that balance personalization with privacy and human autonomy. Drawing on both established engineering practices and emerging ethical frameworks, we examine how ethical constraints can serve as drivers of better system design, rather than limitations on innovation.We discuss how to build agents that learn from individual behavior while actively promoting accountability, collaboration, and well-being, without crossing into exploitation, surveillance, or intrusion. Central to this approach is an understanding that AI systems are not just computational tools, but participants in human communication.

events.title: The Sanity Check: Observability for AI Data Pipelines - David Aronchick, Expanso
events.description: Your AI just confidently gave a completely wrong answer. Why? Was it a bad model? Or was it poisoned by garbage data ingested three weeks ago from a rogue Salesforce update? You have no idea, because your AI data pipeline is a complete black box.This is a talk about lighting up that black box. We (from the teams that built Kubernetes and scaled Kinesis) will show you how to apply the hard-won SRE principles from hyper-scale data systems to your AI infrastructure. This isn't about model monitoring; it's about data pipeline observability. We'll show you the architecture and tools required to trace a single bad prediction all the way back to the specific source data that caused it.You'll leave knowing how to monitor for data freshness, track lineage from source to token, and alert on data drift before it silently corrupts your entire system.

events.title: Expl(AI)n Like I’m 5: An Introduction To AI-Native Networking - Niranjan Shankar, Microsoft & Ram Vennam, Solo.io
events.description: With new AI protocols, acronyms, and networking tools proliferating across the ecosystem, it’s easy to feel overwhelmed. What do all these new terms actually mean, and which ones are relevant to you? Do you really need to overhaul your networking infrastructure, or are traditional solutions sufficient?In this session, we’ll explore the communications patterns behind core AI use-cases - training, inference, serving, consumption, and agentic interactions. You’ll learn the basics of key concepts and features, like MCP, A2A, token-based rate-limiting, prompt guarding, etc, and where they apply. We’ll then discuss where conventional networking technologies - service mesh, CNIs, gateways, and L7 proxies - fit into this paradigm, and when AI-aware implementations like the Gateway API Inference Extension are required for routing, observing, and securing GenAI workloads. Finally, we’ll end with practical guidelines for taking these AI tools to production and integrating them into your stack.

events.title: Smarter Databases in Kubernetes: Applying AI for Query Optimization and Self-Tuning - Peter Zaitsev, Percona
events.description: As databases increasingly power real-time, data-intensive applications, managing their performance within Kubernetes environments presents new challenges. This session explores how artificial intelligence can transform traditional database management through adaptive query optimization and self-tuning capabilities.We’ll discuss practical approaches for integrating AI and machine learning models into cloud-native database platforms to analyze workloads, detect inefficiencies, and automatically adjust parameters such as indexing, caching, and resource allocation. The talk will highlight emerging patterns behind “self-driving” databases, systems that learn from historical performance data to improve query efficiency and response times continuously.Attendees will gain actionable insights into building smarter, autonomous database systems that leverage AI to optimize performance, reduce operational overhead, and enhance scalability across Kubernetes clusters.

events.title: Panel: Routing Intelligence Vs Traffic Control: Architectural Tradeoffs for AI Inference in Gateway API - Abdullah Gharaibeh, Google; Morgan Foster, Red Hat; Eitan Yarmush, Solo.io; Nili Guy, IBM, Dan Sun, Bloomberg
events.description: As AI inference workloads become the norm in Kubernetes, the Gateway API Inference Extension (GAIE) faced a pivotal architectural question: should request scheduling act as a "consultant" via an external processing filter or become a full-fledged Service in the traffic path?This panel brings together maintainers and practitioners to explore two models for routing AI inference. GAIE uses external processing to make scheduling decisions without proxying traffic - an unconventional but deliberate choice that prioritizes simplicity and performance. An alternative would place the scheduler directly in the data path as a next-hop Service, aligning with Gateway API norms.This discussion isn’t just theoretical - the architecture shapes what GAIE can do and how it integrates with Gateway API. Attendees will leave with a clear decision framework for designing GW API extensions, and a deeper understanding of how architectural choices impact performance, complexity, and ecosystem alignment.

events.title: Rollout on Reception: Progressive Delivery for GenAI Apps Using Real-Time User Feedback - Thomas Vitale, Systemic & Whitney Lee, Data Dog
events.description: When AI-generated text goes straight to users, how do you know whether it's good? Not error rate, not latency, not health checks, but a new dimension: user feedback. So what if that feedback could directly control how new app versions roll out? Not through A/B test dashboards, but through real-time GenAI evaluation?In this session, Whitney and Thomas demo a cloud-native platform where Knative Services and Flagger drive progressive delivery using live GenAI evaluation results to route traffic. They show how to integrate cloud native apps with inference services and how to instrument everything with OpenTelemetry following the Generative AI Semantic Conventions. Then user satisfaction controls whether a new version of an app gets rolled out.And for this talk, YOU'RE the user. You'll evaluate competing app versions live, upvote the best responses, and watch as your feedback directly shifts traffic to the winner in real time.Join and learn how to pit prompt against prompt or model against model and let your users decide what outcome they truly like best.

events.title: Baby Sitting Your AI Models in Production? STOP! - Ram Iyengar, Linux Foundation
events.description: AI in production is broken. They're as fussy, as can be. The number of personas involved in making an AI model ready for production is high, which makes MLOps an order of magnitude more complicated compared to DevOps.This talk introduces a streamlined, standardized workflow to take the manual effort out of MLOps packaging & deployment.The first step is ModelPack, a sandbox project under the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). It provides a standard, open-source way to organize all the files that make up a machine learning model.We will introduce ModelKit, which is a directory structure that logically groups all the previously dispersed artifacts into a single, cohesive package. The final deployment artifact will automate the process of building a runnable, OCI-compliant container image.The final step of deployment becomes rudimentary, as the image is available on any container registry.

events.title: Kubeflow Trainer Observability: Real-Time Progress Tracking for Reliable ML Training on Kubernetes - Abhijeet Dhumal & Rob Bell, Red Hat
events.description: Without observability, training is just expensive guesswork.Imagine a hyperparameter search across 50 configurations—16 GPUs for 8 hours each—consumes 6,400 GPU-hours at $25,600. Without real-time metrics, underperforming configs burn resources. Platform teams can't distinguish converging TrainJobs from diverging ones. Algorithms like Hyperband reduce costs through early stopping, but can't function without real-time training metrics from distributed jobs.In this session, we'll show how Kubeflow Trainer v2's native observability unlocks Katib's full potential. We'll demonstrate how training metrics flow into TrainJob status through the Kubernetes API—no code instrumentation, no MLflow servers, no TensorBoard infrastructure. We'll show how to query progress with kubectl. We'll demonstrate Katib's Hyperband watching TrainJob eval metrics and terminating underperforming trials immediately, delivering 5-10x cost reduction.With native observability, make every GPU-hour count!

But executives hesitate to commit to long contracts amid shifting priorities, while researchers dislike losing autoscaling capacity.In this talk, we’ll share how we merged the elasticity of cloud-based autoscaling with the savings of reserved capacity by building a custom platform on Kubernetes - one that orchestrates researchers’ training workloads, abstracts away infrastructure complexity, and keeps users informed. We’ll walk through the architecture and core capabilities, from transparent, adaptive preemption guided by team priorities to automated coordination with hardware providers for optimal resolution of hardware issues and dynamic budgeting that enables bursting into on-demand GPUs when needed.If you’re exploring how to make cloud-native AI research infrastructure both cost-efficient and researcher-friendly, this session is for you.

events.title: Real-Time GPU Job Scheduling Latency Prediction in Multi-Cluster Kubernetes - Sujoy Dutta, Bloomberg
events.description: Managing thousands of GPU-enabled jobs across a multi-cluster Kubernetes environment can create unpredictable scheduling delays, impacting productivity and resource utilization. Bloomberg uses Karmada to manage ML and training workloads across data centers. This talk describes a production-ready Karmada controller that improves GPU scheduling with real-time queueing predictions, transforming a laggy process into an efficient experience.Our system delivers precise queuing estimates through immediate processing of resource events and intelligent Karmada integration. Internal users can better understand job wait times with improved accuracy, enabling better planning and reduced GPU idle times. Our solution combines real-time statistics with Kubernetes events, seamlessly integrating with monitoring tools like Grafana and Prometheus. Attendees will learn how these innovations provide real-time insights with O(1) complexity, enhancing prediction accuracy from daily averages to live metrics.

Running your own infrastructure can help tackle these problems, but it requires a platform to host the challenges and run the submissions. To run this platform, a framework using Kubernetes, Codabench and KubeFlow will be presented for running particle physics-based challenges at CERN.

events.title: Kubernetes Meets Climate Data: Designing Analysis-Ready Data Cubes With Kubeflow and Zarr - Armagan Karatosun & Anna-Lena Erdmann, EUMETSAT
events.description: The exponential growth of Earth Observation (EO) data has transformed how the climate community processes and analyzes data. In previous years, projects like the European Weather Cloud (EWC) and Data Proximate Compute (DPC) enabled researchers to bring compute closer to data across multi-cloud environments. The next challenge is making these massive datasets analysis-ready for analytics, ML, and AI.But the biggest obstacle lies in the EO data itself—spread across archives, stored in multiple formats (HDF5, NetCDF, GRIB), and organized into layers (L1–L3) with varying spatial and temporal resolutions. This fragmentation makes efficient access, reprocessing, and analysis extremely difficult.This session presents how we built a cloud-native data pipeline using Kubeflow, DuckDB, and Xarray/Zarr to transform EO data into analysis-ready, multi-resolution data cubes. Attendees will learn how Kubeflow orchestrates large-scale scientific data preparation and object-store-native access.

events.title: When Models Write Malware: Defending Against On-demand Exploits - Shane Lawrence, Shopify
events.description: AI has made it easy for even amateur attackers to generate exploits on the fly, and skilled hackers can iterate on initial access to produce targeted custom scripts in seconds. How do we detect purpose-built malware that can evolve faster than static signatures can track?In this talk, Shane Lawrence will perform a live demo of an attack and then show how to pair Falco runtime signals with an LLM-based script classifier to spot malicious intent in near real time.Attendees can expect to leave with an understanding of how AI has changed the field for attackers and defenders, techniques for hardening containers against such attacks, and a reference implementation for detection complete with prompts and helm charts for leveraging AI and open source tools.

events.title: A Deep Dive on How To Leverage the NVIDIA GB200 for Ultra-Fast Training and Inference on Kubernetes - Kevin Klues & Jan-Philip Gehrcke, NVIDIA
events.description: Multi-node AI training and inference jobs split workloads across nodes to scale performance and handle large datasets. With the introduction of specialized hardware such as NVIDIA’s GB200 NVL72, these jobs are no longer limited by traditional networking fabrics such as InfiniBand or RoCE. Instead, NVLink connects all 72 GPUs across 18 nodes in a full mesh network, accelerating multi-node workloads with low-latency, high-bandwidth communication.In this talk, we introduce a new abstraction called a “ComputeDomain,” which users create via NVIDIA’s DRA driver for GPUs to run multi-node workloads on NVLink-connected GB200 systems. We discuss the intricacies of what’s needed to run these workloads securely, as well as how the ComputeDomain abstraction leverages DRA to hide these details from the end-user. Additionally, we discuss how this support has been pushed to all major cloud providers and integrated with their managed Kubernetes offerings. We conclude with a demo.

events.description: This talk is a personal journey that began with a simple desire: to make life easier as a developer assigned to managing recurring Kubernetes operational issues. Faced with frequent pod failures, node assignment bottlenecks, and the challenge of safeguarding formations from accidental deletions, I initiated and built a proof-of-concept pipeline leveraging Langflow's visual orchestration and GROQ language for contextual LLM querying. This AI-driven system automates the detection, diagnosis, & remediation of common customer issues by combining Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), cloud-native Astra DB vector search, and multi-turn conversational interaction, accelerating ticket resolution and improving cluster stability without overwhelming human operators. The talk shares this journey, the system architecture, and how this approach embodies a practical application of generative AI & RAG in Kubernetes operations, fitting naturally in the evolving Cloud Native AI and Kubeflow ecosystem.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Roche's Kubeflow Story: Large Models in Seconds, Costs Cut, Security up With Bottlerocket - Oswaldo Gomez, Roche
events.description: At Roche pRED, our MLOps platform runs hundreds of ML endpoints for scientific discovery. Spikes in traffic were causing timeouts, as our large (13.4GB+) GPU models took ~10 minutes for a cold-start pull. To ensure quick responses, we were forced to set min=1 replicas, resulting in over $100K in annual waste for idle nodes. This was unsustainable, especially as we retrain and deploy new models daily.This is our end-user story of how we solved this by adopting Karpenter for rapid node autoscaling and Bottlerocket on EKS. We will detail the specific technique of using Bottlerocket's mutable data volume to prefetch large container images before pods are scheduled.We will show load-test data (20K API calls) that proves how we cut pull times from minutes to seconds, achieving a 58% faster cold-start. Learn how this combination enabled true scale-to-zero (even for GPUs), slashed costs, and enhanced security with an immutable OS

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Intelligent Traffic Routing for Distributed LLM Inference: Beyond Traditional Gateway Approaches - Zhonghu Xu, Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd
events.description: As LLM inference adopts Kubernetes, intelligent routing has become critical. Existing gateways like Gateway Inference Extension, LLM-d, and Aibrix struggle with emerging patterns like prefill-decode (PD) disaggregation and distributed parallelism (DP+EP).This session introduces **Kthena Router**, a production-grade orchestration system for multi-model LLM workloads. Unlike approaches relying solely on engine metrics, it uses **closed-loop control with adaptive modeling** based on connections, token lengths, load distribution, and role-aware routing.In this session, we also will deep dive:1. How to do multi-model serving through routing policies, eliminating per-model gateway deployments2. Native PD disaggregation support with prefill-decode awareness, then removes dependencies on per-group routers or LLM-d sidecars3. Pluggable scheduling with fairness scheduling, semantic-aware routing, KV-cache aware placement, and GPU utilization-aware balancing.

As a result, resource utilization becomes a critical concern.In this talk, we will share solutions and practices for implementing GPU sharing in Kubernetes, focusing on two key projects gaining traction recently: Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) and the CNCF sandbox project HAMi. The presentation will cover the following topics:1. Challenges in GPU sharing.2. Approaches for sharing AI chips beyond NVIDIA GPUs.3. How sharing technologies integrate with projects like Volcano, Koordinator, and Kueue.

events.description: For about a decade, the telco industry has been perfecting the “Cloud-Native” playbook—moving from rigid hardware to flexible, software-defined ecosystems. But as we move into 2026, agility is no longer enough; we need autonomy. This keynote explores the industry’s next chapter of moving from cloud-native foundations to an Agentic-AI-Native future.We’ll look at the big picture: how the next generation of networks will transition from running on code to “thinking” through autonomous agents. Discover why an open-source-first strategy is the essential bridge for this transition, ensuring that as our networks become smarter, they remain open, interoperable, and ready for the era of self-healing infrastructure.

events.title: The Autonomous Telco: AI Agents as First-Class Citizens in Cloud-Native Infrastructure- Samy Nitsche & Dorian Lenzner - Deutsche Telekom
events.description: Network troubleshooting in cloud-native telco environments requires expert knowledge of complex protocols (NGAP, PFCP, GTP) and hours of manual analysis. This talk introduces KubeTrace, a distributed packet capture system for Kubernetes that integrates AI agents into the observability stack via the Model Context Protocol (MCP).We'll demonstrate how KubeTrace enables continuous packet capture with AI-friendly interfaces. You'll see AI agents diagnosing 5G core issues—from UE registration failures to inter-NF communication problems—by autonomously correlating packets across multiple interfaces and nodes in seconds.The second half explores AI disruption in telco DevOps: automated security testing, CI/CD pipelines where AI generates test cases from production traffic, self-healing systems and continuous compliance auditing.Learn from real-world Deutsche Telekom experience building AI-first observability and witness how network engineers become orchestrators of AI operations.

events.title: Teaching Machines To Debug Machines: Building an AI-Powered RCA Agent for Sylva-Core - Mihai Zaharia & Vladimir Braquet, Orange
events.description: In modern cloud-native environments, operators face hundreds of alerts daily from Kubernetes clusters, network functions, and infrastructure components. Traditional approaches require manual investigation, correlation across multiple tools, and, let's face it, tribal knowledge that doesn't quite scale well.This talk focuses on our journey building an RCA agent that autonomously investigates and tries to get the root cause of alerts in a Kubernetes-based telco cloud environment.As the base infrastructure we used `sylva-core` to deploy our k8s clusters and manage the monitoring stack.We'll go through the steps that we took to build this , focusing on:- Choosing the right framework .- Prompt engineering and design patterns.- Model Context Protocol- Memory systemsWe'll walk through real examples of the agent investigating Kubernetes pod failures, correlating them with similar past incidents, and providing actionable RCA without human intervention.

The talk argues that true reliability isn’t about tools or automation alone — it’s about operational discipline, teamwork, and engineering for the unpredictable.

events.title: Extracting the Last Microseconds of Performance From Containerized Workloads - Feruzjon Muyassarov, Ericsson Software Technology & Alexander Kanevskiy, Intel
events.description: Cooking a perfect low-latency containerized environment is made of three ingredients: choosing the best combination of resources, spicing them up, and minimizing competition over them. While the first two ingredients ensure achieving the lowest possible latency, the last two make it very predictable. This talk gives both a broad overview of what really matters, and also ready-to-use recipes to try with your own containers.In addition to a walk through of established technology like isolated CPUs, memory and device locality, and cache and memory bandwidth allocation, this talk covers in more detail very recent enhancements in container runtimes and their effects. For instance, combining two new features, container-specific Linux scheduler and CPU power saving state controls, one can reduce processes’ wake-up latency even by 99 % in some occasions.

Mitigating agentic app risks drives a new Telco Agentic AI infrastructure, extending NFV to handle agentic workloads with secure sandbox runtimes.Cloud native ecosystems benefit by incorporating agentic AI via secured virtualized containers, speeding up telecom cloud and AI-native infrastructure rollout. Hybrid, end-to-end orchestration offers a holistic view of future telecom networks.In this panel, telecom operators, standards bodies, and open source communities share views on AI agent infrastructure implementation and cloud native synergies.

events.title: What Do You Mean It’s Broken? When AI Meets the Harsh Reality of Telco Observability - Piotr Matysiak & Iwona Wojdan, Orange
events.description: In Telco cloud environments, finding the root cause across many Kubernetes clusters can be like searching in the dark—traditional observability tools provide data, but not actionable insights.We’ll introduce an AI-driven observability approach, where an intelligent agent augments classic telemetry analysis with reasoning capabilities. The system combines live telemetry (logs, metrics) with static knowledge sources (standards, vendor documentation) to deliver context-aware answers.We’ll walk through the proposed architecture and its dynamic query optimization decision layer—a mechanism that determines the most efficient strategy to resolve each user query. You’ll see how it integrates with multiple Kubernetes clusters and how it enhances a Telco AI Assistant, an agentic system supporting lifecycle management (LCM) of 5G/6G networks via natural language.Let’s stop pretending data will just appear—AI and automation are only as good as the observability feeding them.

events.description: NGMN's Cloud Native Manifesto defines a north star for telecom, but transforming principles into practice requires more than vision—it demands measurable criteria, collaboration, and testable outcomes.The Cloud Native Telecom Initiative (CNTi) provides exactly this bridge. Through its open-source Test Suite, the project codifies best practices for CNFs, turning manifesto values like portability, scalability, and automation into verifiable checks. This collaboration offers operators and vendors an ecosystem-wide framework: from the cloud infrastructure to the CNF itself.Learning Objectives- How CNTi and Sylva collaborate to create a telecom cloud native framework- How NGMN's Cloud Native Manifesto inspires practical, testable criteria- How operators and vendors can leverage Sylva and CNTi to accelerate their cloud native transformations.

events.title: Air France–KLM’s GitOps Takeoff: Real Stories From the Flight Deck - Alaoui Echerif Amine & Ravi Ramrattan, Air France KLM
events.description: When Air France–KLM started its GitOps journey, the biggest challenge wasn’t the tool, it was THE TRUST. The Teams were used to centralized operations, manual gates, and direct control over production systems. Moving to GitOps meant automation, transparency, and letting Git drive the change process. That shift raised questions: Would we lose control? Could teams make safe changes on their own without platform owner?This talk shares how we navigated those fears and turned them into a model for shared ownership. Using Flux and CI/CD, we built a GitOps foundation that gives CyberSecurity, Observability, and Network Engineering teams full autonomy to manage their tools, all within a secure and auditable framework.Why attend this talk?Because it shows how a small idea from a small internal team around open source tooling can actually shift the direction of a large organization. It’s about starting small, proving value fast, and turning that into real cultural and technical change.

events.title: Towards Better Canary Releases With Flagger and Gateway API - Sanskar Jaiswal, Kong
events.description: Canary releases are a great way to bring new software into production as they help identify any problems early and avert disasters. However, existing strategies of weighted traffic shifting result in a bad user experience as requests randomly bounce between old and new versions on a per-request basis. It also does not produce great signal for developers. Can we have better Canary signals while improving end user experience?In this talk, Sanskar will demo a relatively new deployment strategy that modifies the Canary release workflow to work with user sessions rather than user requests. He will describe how session-based routing results in a more robust Canary deployment system, allowing servers to be stateless while providing a consistent user experience. He will discuss how Gateway API plays a crucial role in enabling this feature and conclude the session with a demo showcasing this new strategy.

events.title: Talking to Your Cluster: Conversational GitOps with Flux MCP - William Rizzo, Mirantis
events.description: The Flux project is pioneering a new era, evolving GitOps from simple deployment automation into an intelligent operations platform. This session explores how AI-assisted GitOps transforms daily work by connecting conversational interfaces to Flux, enabling instant health insights, clearer understanding of delivery pipelines, and rapid detection of configuration drift across clusters. We’ll highlight how AI accelerates incident response, generates architecture documentation on demand, and supports secure developer self-service. Finally, we’ll preview how this model paves the way for advanced capabilities like staged multi-cluster rollouts, predictive troubleshooting, and policy-driven automation.
