events.title: Advancing Machine to Machine Workflows with the SPIFFE Identity Provider - Andrew Block, Red Hat & Maia Iyer, IBM Research
events.description: Most Keycloak users start by configuring realms, clients, and roles by hand—but that doesn’t scale. As organizations adopt multi-tenant and cloud-native applications, manual configuration becomes a bottleneck and a source of security drift.This session shows an approach for making Keycloak configuration declarative and repeatable. We’ll explore how to represent Keycloak realms and clients as code, how to bootstrap client credentials automatically, and how to connect Keycloak with trusted workload identities using SPIFFE/SPIRE so you'll never worry about copying a client secret ever again.You’ll see real-world patterns for automating Keycloak setup across environments, including how to reconcile configuration drift, enforce consistent RBAC policies, and safely manage client registration at scale. By the end, you’ll know how to make Keycloak management as easy—and as reliable—as any modern DevOps workflow.

events.title: The Keycloak-Token-Configuration Mistake, 90% of All Developers Do (and How To Avoid It) - Niko Köbler IT-Beratung
events.description: You deploy Keycloak, everything works—or so you think. But there's a problem that almost everyone misses: Keycloak makes it deliberately easy for developers. By default, it puts a lot of information into your tokens: roles, claims, metadata. At first, this feels good because everything "just works."But this comes with a price:🚨 Your tokens contain more data than necessary🚨 Recipients receive information they don't need or shouldn't know🚨 Data protection principles are not optimally implemented🚨 HTTP requests can fail due to tokens that are too largeIn this talk, you will learn:✅ Why Keycloak reveals “too much of a good thing” by default✅ Which token contents are really necessary (and which are not)✅ How to implement data minimization through targeted configuration✅ Practical settings and strategies for lean and secure tokensThe result:Privacy-compliant tokens that contain only what the recipient needs—no more, no less. Security by design instead of by chance.

events.description: Mission-critical railway management systems are increasingly interconnected, making cybersecurity more essential than ever.We, at Hitachi Rail, have therefore rebuilt our products using secure-by-design principles, with Keycloak serving as a central component of our architecture.Keycloak has also supported us in modernizing the operation and maintenance of our IT systems, running these management systems in the customers data-centers.Regulations such as NIS2 and the CRA confirm that we are well positioned for the challenges ahead.This talk highlights some IEC 62443-4-2 requirements that often challenge traditional IT architectures, and demonstrates how Keycloak addresses them effectively.Although Keycloak alone does not guarantee full compliance, it plays a key role in realizing resilient, modern secure-by-design architectures.

events.description: In the MCP community, Enterprise-Managed Authorization for MCP is proposed as Specification Enhancement Proposal #646(SEP-646, https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/pull/646), which was proposed by one of Auth 2.1 spec authors and has just been merged as MCP Authorization Extensions on Oct 31st.This proposal is intended to provide a solution for the authorization problem of MCP in enterprise use case and makeMCP authorization enterprise-ready from both ux and security perspective.The target problem is that users need to manually log in and consent to grant an Al agent access to each external resources one by one and it is hard to visualize those access enough for enterprise security.It will be getting bigger with future explosion of Al agents.The session will cover the following:- ﻿﻿the target problem and the solution of SEP-646- ﻿﻿Keycloak’s implementation for RFC7523, which is prerequisite spec for SEP-646- implementation of SEP-646 using Keycloak

events.title: Bridging the Gap Between Human and Workload Identity: SPIFFE Support in Keycloak - Arndt Schwenkschuster, Defakto Security & Dmitry Telegin, Backbase UK
events.description: Human identity has been around for a while. In the beginning, authentication was just a simple password. With second-factor authentication and lately passkeys this has become much more secure but there’s always a factor of customer experience. How many factors can you prompt until the user stops?This is different with workloads. They will endlessly provide authentication factors and won’t complain. This is great for security and building “multi-factor authentication” for workloads is easy. Keycloak supports multiple client authentication methods but many are difficult to deploy. Most of the time, simple client secret authentication is chosen. Others require key pairs and issuing self-signed JSON Web Tokens.Since Keycloak 26.4, secure client authentication is easier than ever. Support for SPIFFE identities as a means of client authentication has been added. No more client secrets or difficult-to-deploy secret keys. You can simply use a SPIFFE identity obtained at runtime.

events.description: You have built your first MCP server and tested it with the MCP Inspector, but it currently runs over stdio or streamable HTTP without HTTPS. Now you are asking yourself: should you rewrite your server to add authentication and authorization, or is there a smarter approach? And what happens when you scale to multiple MCP servers? Do you really need to modify each one?In this demo-driven session, Lin first shows the "hard way": modifying MCP server code, rebuilding, and redeploying to add authentication. After that, she introduces a much simpler method by offloading authentication to Keycloak through an MCP gateway, which allows you to secure your servers without changing or redeploying them.

events.description: In a world of constant connectivity, air-gapped systems may feel like relics - yet they remain critical in defense, national labs, and high-assurance zones.In this talk, we invert the usual paradigm: focusing on the identity management problem space in a disconnected world. Starting with Zarf, we’ll demonstrate how to construct sealed, self-contained delivery bundles. We then dive deep into how we transformed Keycloak - from custom authentication and authorization flows with CAC, to custom client policies, and up to Client Authenticators for Kubernetes. Next, we will explore how our platform meets DoD-level requirements, such as securing the supply chain, providing SBOMs, and enabling full network lockdown with the help of Istio and Authservice.By the end of the session, you’ll have a tactical plan to deploy Keycloak in environments where “always online” is forbidden - and a hardened reference architecture you can apply to your next mission-critical deployment.

Feeding the Cloud: Designing Data-Driven Robotics Systems - Nina Polshakova, Solo.io & Aaron Ray, MIT
events.description: You might imagine a robot as a standalone entity, like R2-D2, with all processing onboard. In reality, modern robotics systems often send vast amounts of data to cloud backends for processing and training, involving complex networking and distributed computing.While edge devices and IoT also combine cloud and local computation, robots face unpredictable connectivity, requiring failover to local compute and smaller models. Determining which computations should run on-device versus in the cloud presents rich research opportunities, and distributing computation adds networking and orchestration complexity. Effective system design must carefully balance the efficiency of local computation with the greater power of cloud-based reasoning.This session explores how cloud-native, distributed architectures can coordinate edge and cloud computation to enable reliable, intelligent robotics in real-world environments.

events.title: Making Observability Work at the Edge - Julia Furst Morgado, Dash0 & Graziano Casto, Akamas
events.description: Observability gets tricky when you take Kubernetes to the Edge. Sending full traces and metrics upstream doesn’t scale when every byte and millisecond count.This talk introduces an ultra-optimized OpenTelemetry Micro-Collector strategy built for Edge environments. It demonstrates how to minimize overhead without losing visibility by applying smart data reduction techniques like Tail-Based Sampling and Lossless Batching directly on the device, ensuring that only high-value telemetry such as errors and slow requests leave the Edge.The session covers how to deploy a lightweight DaemonSet tuned for K3s or MicroK8s using the OpenTelemetry Collector Contrib, integrate Fluent Bit for unified log ingestion, and maintain data integrity during disconnections. It shows what it takes to make observability truly sustainable in constrained Edge environments.

events.description: As AI workloads move closer to users, devices, and data sources, the edge has become the next proving ground — yet expectations often outpace operational reality. Drawing on insights from a recently released industry research paper on Edge AI, this talk explores what is actually happening as teams deploy and operate AI at the edge.We’ll walk through real-world data on infrastructure constraints, workload placement decisions, model lifecycle management, and operational tradeoffs observed across production edge environments. The session examines where Kubernetes-based approaches enable progress, where they introduce friction, and how architectures are evolving to support reliable, scalable AI inference beyond the datacenter.The talk focuses on recurring patterns and practical considerations that are shaping current edge AI deployments and influencing how teams think about future designs.

events.description: Edge AI is transforming operations by bringing inference and AI agents closer to where data is generated. From real-time decisions on factory floors to low-latency insights in retail, energy, and transportation, running AI at the edge enables faster responses, lower bandwidth costs, and increased resilience. However, operating AI across highly distributed, resource-constrained environments introduces significant complexity. This session explores how Kubernetes is a critical foundation for running AI at the edge, enabling consistent deployment, orchestration, and lifecycle management of AI agents and workloads across thousands of locations while maintaining reliability and security. Through real-world examples, Said will highlight proven architectures, best practices, and common pitfalls when combining edge AI and Kubernetes, equipping teams to confidently operationalize AI at the edge.

events.title: IoT Compute Unleashed: Transparent Offload From ESP32 To Edge Clusters - Anastassios Nanos & Charalampos Mainas, Nubis PC
events.description: How can a ESP32 microcontroller leverage Kubernetes for compute acceleration? This talk showcases how a tiny microcontroller can run inference on a full YOLOv11 model in less than 200ms! We leverage vAccel, an open abstraction layer that allows IoT devices like the ESP32 to transparently offload ML inference and signal-processing tasks to nearby edge or cloud nodes. Through a lightweight agent and a unified API, vAccel bridges heterogeneous devices, enabling seamless offload without modifying application logic. The session demonstrates a real deployment where an ESP32 delegates inference to a GPU-backed Kubernetes node, reducing latency and power consumption while preserving Kubernetes-native control and observability. Attendees will see how open acceleration can unify the Cloud–Edge–IoT stack through standard interfaces and reusable runtimes.

events.description: As Kubernetes expands beyond the cloud into disconnected and resource-constrained environments, integrity must be proven, not assumed.This session presents a reproducible framework for verifiable software supply chains, combining SLSA-compliant builds, signed artefacts, and GitOps automation.Using open-source tools such as cosign, SignServer, and Flux/ArgoCD, we'll show how hardware-backed keys (eg YubiKeys) can anchor cryptographic trust directly into CI/CD workflows.Attendees will learn how to achieve continuous attestation from build to deployment, ensuring reproducible, signed, and verifiable workloads, anywhere Kubernetes runs.

events.title: Building Unified Global Load Balancing for the Edge With k8gb -Bradley Andersen, k8gb & Yury Tsarev, Upbound
events.description: As Kubernetes expands to hundreds or thousands of edge locations across retail, telecom, and industrial sites, traditional GSLB tools fall short. Proprietary, data-center-centric systems are hard to run where connectivity is intermittent, autonomy is needed, and latency targets are strict.This session demonstrates how k8gb, a CNCF Sandbox project, brings global load balancing into the Kubernetes model. Using a simple CRD and GitOps workflow, k8gb leverages DNS as a reliable, standards-based mechanism while adding Kubernetes-native automation and observability. The speaker shows patterns such as nearest-edge routing, traffic spillover, and graceful degradation, and explains how k8gb detects and reports inconsistent DNS state during partitions so operators can restore a consistent global view once connectivity returns.Attendees will take away practical patterns for health-based failover, geo-aware routing, and disaster recovery at the edge.

events.title: Policy & GitOps Unite! Kyverno and Flux Save Cluster City - Cortney Nickerson, Nirmata & Leigh Capili, ControlPlane
events.description: In the metropolis of Cluster City, chaos lurks in every deployment. Rogue CVEs creep through shadows, and Entropy, master of drift, bends clusters away from Git-defined truth. But fear not! Two heroes stand tall: Kylie Kyverno, guardian of Policy-as-Code, and Flux Mason, defender of GitOps order.With their rhythmic sidekick Emcee Percy (MCP), this dynamic duo takes on the forces of misconfiguration and drift. Watch as unauthorized changes threaten the peace and learn how to:Establish policy-driven guardrails to prevent configuration chaosCoordinate automated rollbacks & reconciliations for trusted productionProvide real-time visibility and reporting through MCP dashboards & eventsTurn policy violations into actionable signals to close the GitOps loopAttendees will walk away understanding how Kyverno and Flux complement each other to maintain consistent, compliant, and resilient infrastructure. Join us! Be guardians of GitOps harmony with these powerful open source tools.

events.title: Self-Healing Clusters: When Kyverno Writes Its Own Rules - Johan Heimar Prieto Benavides, CursaCloud & Oscar Castillo, Genius Sports
events.description: What if your cluster could fix its own mistakes? In this talk, we’ll share how we used Kyverno not only to enforce policies but to generate new ones based on real-time drift and audit feedback.By analyzing repeated violations, our platform automatically suggested corrective policies that operators could review and approve. The result: a self-evolving governance system where prevention improves itself over time.We’ll discuss the architecture behind adaptive policies, the telemetry pipelines that feed them, and how this approach reduced incidents and configuration drift across multiple environments.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Compliance as Code Meets Developer Portals: Kyverno + Backstage in Action - Gaurika Poplai, Microsoft
events.description: This session explores how integrating Kyverno with Backstage brings Kubernetes compliance directly into the developer workflow. Instead of treating compliance as an operations-driven task, Kyverno’s policy results can be surfaced inside Backstage through the Policy Reporter plugin, giving developers immediate visibility into how their workloads align with CIS benchmarks and organizational policies. Using Kyverno and Policy Reporter, the session demonstrates how policy results from running clusters can be visualized per service, showing pass/fail status, violated rules, and historical trends—all within the Backstage service catalog. Through a live demo, attendees will see how developers can identify failed policies, understand their compliance posture, and take proactive action before deployment. By shifting compliance visibility left, this approach transforms governance from a reactive process into a shared, continuous responsibility embedded in the developer experience.

events.title: Beyond Admission: How Kyverno Extends Native Policies for Full Lifecycle Management - Erik Godding Boye, Zenior AS & Mariam Fahmy, Cloudflare
events.description: Your Kubernetes admission policies are guarding the front door, but what about the threats already inside? Native policies leave your existing workloads in a dangerous blind spot and offer no way to test changes without risking production. It's time to upgrade your defenses.This session explores these critical gaps, demonstrating how Kyverno provides a comprehensive, lifecycle-aware solution. We'll show you how to “shift left” by validating policies against your cluster state before they're ever deployed, ensuring safe rollouts. We'll then cover mutation from two angles: first, using Kyverno's MutatingPolicy for admission-time changes on any Kubernetes version. Then, we leap beyond admission control to demonstrate Kyverno's game-changing ability to find non-compliant resources already running in your cluster, a power that native policies simply don’t have.

events.title: Full Observability for Kyverno With Observability-as-Code - Imma Valls, Grafana Laba
events.description: The adoption of Kyverno solves the challenge of Policy-as-Code. However, a new question emerges for platform engineers: what happens when a slow policy rule adds latency? A slow admission webhook can degrade performance across the k8s cluster.Tracing the webhook's lifecycle is the fastest way to pinpoint any bottlenecking rule.The next step in your GitOps journey is to make your monitoring as declarative as your policies. In the live demo, you’ll learn how to monitor Kyverno's operational cycle using Prometheus, Tempo, and Grafana. We'll set SLOs and enable real-time alerts with observability as code, including metrics and alerts for policy failure rates or slow webhooks, and utilizing OpenTelemetry tracing to pinpoint what is causing a delay in the admission process.If you manage policies as code, don't let silent performance bottlenecks threaten your cluster stability. Join to learn concrete steps needed to close your GitOps loop and achieve operational control over Kyverno.

events.title: Kyverno Mutating Policies: Shifting Platform Logic Into the Control Plane - Rodrigo Fior Kuntzer, Miro
events.description: Ever wondered how to empower developers while maintaining tight operational control and security? This session delves into Kyverno's mutating policies, the ultimate tool for modern platform engineering. Learn to shift complex automation logic down into the Kubernetes control plane, ensuring every resource adheres to your standards before it even lands in the cluster. We will analyze production-grade use cases, including:- Intelligent image registry mapping for global deployments, ensuring secure, fast, and regionalized image pulls (eliminating egress costs).- Dynamic injection of configuration secrets, sidecars, and architecture affinities based on resource context.- Centralized configuration management for autoscaling endpoints, decoupled from application manifests.Discover the secrets to running these critical policies in highly available, GitOps-managed clusters, ensuring stability and preventing configuration drift.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Know Your Policy Level: A Lightning Introduction - Danielle Cook, Akamai & Simon Forster, Stackegy
events.description: In just 10 minutes, we will rapid fire through the Cloud Native Maturity Model’s Policy Maturity track. Think of it as a quick gut check for your organization. Do you have policy for cloud native? Are you translating rules from legacy systems and trying to retrofit them? Are you copy and pasting and hoping for the best? Or is everything humming along with full automation?This session gives attendees a simple way to reflect on where they are as they move through KyvernoCon. You will get a fast framework to talk with your teams and leaders about what progress looks like, why policy is central to cloud native success, and how to keep leveling up.It is also a call to action. The maturity model is growing, but policy needs more real world input. We want your stories, your strange corner cases, and your expertise to help strengthen it for the entire community. If you are new to the model, this is a friendly place to start. If you know it well, come help shape what comes next.

events.title: Beyond Match & Pattern: Mastering Kyverno with CEL- Koray Oksay
events.description: Kyverno’s YAML-first policy model is easy to adopt for Kubernetes users, but as complexity grows, traditional match and pattern rules can become flaky or overly verbose. CEL (Common Expression Language), a game-changing addition that brings dynamic, context-aware logic, now supports Kyverno policies to overcome this.In this session, you’ll learn how CEL unlocks a new level of flexibility in Kyverno. We will explore how to write cleaner, smarter policies by embedding CEL expressions into validation conditions, preconditions, and match logic. You’ll see real-world use cases where CEL simplifies policy writing, improves performance, and enables previously impossible or challenging tasks in pure YAML.Whether you’re writing policies for security, governance, or multi-tenant control, CEL gives you the power to go beyond boilerplate YAML and build dynamic, context-sensitive guardrails without leaving Kubernetes.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: The < Meta /> Trick: Connecting Page Load To Backend Traces - Wolfgang Therrien, Honeycomb
events.description: You instrument your backend services with distributed tracing. You add OpenTelemetry to your browser application. But there's a gap, your traces start when JavaScript loads, missing everything that happened before: the server-side processing and the initial HTML delivery.Wolfgang shares a useful pattern for embedding the OpenTelemetry trace-parent in a meta tag during HTML generation, then reading it client-side to correlate document load, Core Web Vitals, and errors in a single distributed trace enabling you to query a single complete end-to-end trace for document load.We’ll examine a common debugging scenario where the user reports "The product page feels slow."Without this pattern, you'd see disconnected signals rather than an explicit parent-child relationship. You'd likely waste time trying to correlate those signals or optimizing frontend code when the real fix is on the backend.

events.title: Observability Day | Sponsored Keynote: Beyond Observability: Intelligent Progressive Delivery with Feature Control - Sean O'Dell, Dynatrace
events.description: Observability has traditionally helped teams understand what happened after software was released. But as software delivery accelerates driven by cloud-native architectures, AI-assisted development, and continuous deployment; insight alone is no longer sufficient. The next evolution is beyond observability: closing the loop between insight and action so teams can actively guide software behavior in production, not just observe it.In this talk, we explore how modern platforms are evolving from passive observability toward intelligent software resilience by combining deep runtime context with progressive delivery controls. Over the past several years, the industry has moved steadily “shift-left” and “shift-right,” bringing debugging, performance analysis, security, and compliance closer to both development and production. The result is richer context across the software lifecycle, but also greater responsibility on teams to act quickly and safely when change introduces risk ￼ ￼.Progressive delivery is the missing link. Feature flags and experimentation allow teams to decouple deployment from release, exposing new functionality gradually and selectively. When combined with observability, features become first-class runtime entities—automatically correlated with performance, reliability, security signals, and business outcomes. Instead of asking “what changed?” after an incident, teams can see which feature, for whom, and with what impact in real time.This talk introduces the concept of Intelligent Feature Delivery: progressive rollouts governed continuously by runtime telemetry and AI-driven guardrails. Rather than relying on static rules or manual approvals, the system evaluates live behavior against baselines and objectives. If anomalies appear, rollouts can pause, adjust, or roll back instantly—without redeployment. This creates a closed-loop control system where insight directly drives action, reducing blast radius while increasing delivery velocity ￼.In five minutes, attendees will learn:• Why traditional observability is necessary but insufficient for high-velocity software delivery• How progressive delivery shifts risk management from deployment time to runtime• What it means to treat “the feature” as a unifying concept across engineering, operations, and business• How AI-powered guardrails enable safer experimentation, faster recovery, and clearer business attributionBeyond observability lies a future where platforms don’t just explain system, they actively help operate them. Intelligent progressive delivery is a foundational step toward that future.

events.title: Observability Day | Sponsored Keynote - Before You Let AI Touch Production: Autoremediation Readiness - Colleen White, Chronosphere
events.description: The promise of autoremediation is alluring: through the magic of AI, you  won’t just find issues faster; you’ll resolve them automatically, with nothing left to do  but validate and observe. MTTR becomes obsolete because issues are fixed as they occur.But jumping to this step without a solid foundation of data, curation, and policy is like getting a bad pour from the taps - all froth, no beer.Closed-loop remediation isn’t magic. It’s the final stage in disciplined, AI-driven observability workflow.In this talk, we’ll cover the three essential steps to making autoremediation safe and effective:- Comprehensive system coverage at scale- Usable, navigable data- Rules of the road for autoremediationYou’ll leave with a practical checklist to evaluate your organization’s readiness and framework for deciding how and where autoremediation should (and shouldn’t) be applied.No hype. Just the work required before AI earns the right to act in production.

events.title: A Practical Blueprint for Evolving Observability Schemas - Benedikt Bongartz, Red Hat & Sebastian Rabenhorst, Shopify
events.description: As observability systems grow, evolving telemetry schemas is unavoidable. Even small, backward-incompatible changes can break queries, alerts, duplicate data or harm dashboards. How can we adapt our observability platform to continuous change while preserving stability and performance?In this talk, we’ll explore how the OpenTelemetry Schema evolves and the challenges that come with it. We unpack pain points such as schema upgrades and downgrades (forward and backward compatibility), query-time aliasing and ingest-time translation and show how automating schema evolution can reduce maintenance overhead.We demonstrate practical strategies to overcome these obstacles and walk through Shopify’s approach to safe, automated schema evolution. Attendees will leave with practical ideas for approaching schema evolution in their own observability platforms, reducing maintenance risks, and keeping telemetry pipelines adaptable as they grow.

events.title: Unpacking Your OTel Baggage - Dan Gomez Blanco, New Relic & John Clark, Skyscanner
events.description: When one thinks about OpenTelemetry, the first thoughts that come to mind are traces, metrics, logs, or even profiles. Baggage, as a signal, doesn’t always get the recognition it deserves, but those that have built solutions to propagate domain-specific information from service A to service G via custom headers have learnt that it’s a lot harder than it may initially seem!In this talk, we’ll demystify OTel Baggage as a W3C-standardised, vendor-neutral specification for in-band context propagation. The official "carry-on" for your request context, designed to travel with it everywhere (yes, that pun was very much intended).We'll explore the challenges it solves, some common use cases, and we'll deep-dive into how Skyscanner uses OTel Baggage to propagate Quality of Service (QoS) information. You'll see how this baggage, carrying user priority, is passed down the stack and used by Istio to make intelligent load-shedding decisions.Come claim the context you've been missing!

events.title: How to Use the OpenTelemetry Demo and Why It Matters - Juliano Costa, Datadog
events.description: If you’ve walked by any observability vendor booth at KubeCon in the past couple of years, chances are you’ve seen the OpenTelemetry Demo in action — it’s everywhere. The “Astronomy Shop,” better known as the OpenTelemetry Demo, is more than a collection of microservices — it’s where OpenTelemetry proves itself in action. Widely used by vendors across the ecosystem, it showcases how to apply OpenTelemetry in a real-world scenario: from instrumenting applications to configuring the OpenTelemetry Collector to tie everything together.But the Demo isn’t just for vendors. Whether you’re a newcomer exploring OpenTelemetry for the first time, a SIG maintainer, or an experienced user evaluating an observability backend migration, the Demo can make your life easier and help you do your job better.Join this talk to discover how the OpenTelemetry Demo can empower you by providing the tools and data you need to leverage OpenTelemetry to its full potential!

events.title: Observability at the Edge: State of OpenTelemetry in Kubernetes Ingress Controllers - Kasper Borg Nissen, Dash0
events.description: Ingress controllers are the front door to your Kubernetes cluster, but for many they remain a blind spot. Every request passes through ingress, making it the natural starting point of distributed traces. If you only instrument downstream services, you miss the first span: TLS negotiation, routing decisions, or middleware delays. Without these, your observability story is incomplete.We'll explore the current state of observability across the most widely adopted ingress controllers, showing where OpenTelemetry support is strong, and where gaps remain. We will cover why tracing at the edge is critical, how to combine it with metrics and logs, and how to achieve correlation across signals, e.g. by using the OpenTelemetry Collector to enrich and link telemetry. Through practical insights and live demos, We’ll compare controllers, show how to turn ingress from a black box into the root of your distributed traces, and highlight what this means for the future of observability in Kubernetes.

events.description: From OTLP to OTTL, engineers are excited about a lot of things. But there is one thing that excites them above all else and that is correcting people. Welcome to the second installment of “The Spec-tacular Game Show”.In this fun game show our panelists will be given incorrect statements about the OpenTelemetry Specification or Semantic Conventions. The panelists will buzz in, identify what’s wrong, and state the correction. If none of the panelists know the answer the audience will get a chance to answer to steal the point. The panelist (or audience) with the most points wins!After each question we will discuss why the specification is the way it is and highlight how it produces the production-quality telemetry you know and love. Join us for a fun, relaxing, (snarky) panel about everyone’s favorite part of Otel!

events.title: Scaling Telemetry To Terabytes: Applying Google's Principles To OpenTelemetry With OpAMP - Raphael Menderico, Google
events.description: This talk addresses the challenges of operating Google's monitoring systems at scale, handling terabytes of telemetry data and preventing overload from diverse workloads. We'll explore how Google's internal client library and Monarch, its planet-scale time-series database, work together for cost-effective data collection. Key principles include a distributed push model, dynamic client-side data reduction, centralized retention, and periodic metric analysis. The session will then bridge these concepts to the open-source world, discussing our work with OpenTelemetry's OpAMP protocol to achieve similar scalable and efficient telemetry collection. Attendees will gain insights into adapting these principles for cost savings and learn about our collaboration with the OpAMP SIG to benefit the broader community.

events.title: Accelerating Science, Decelerating Carbon: Sustainable Computing at CERN - Laura Llinares & Amine Lahouel, CERN
events.description: At CERN, where particle physics meets cloud-native scale, workloads are increasingly powered by GPUs and accelerators for simulation and machine learning. These advances are incredible for science—but they raise new questions around efficiency, energy, and carbon impact.We’ll share how we’re building a metrics-driven framework—rooted in Kubernetes and CNCF tooling—to measure and improve workload efficiency across on-prem and cloud environments. By defining a shared set of metrics for cost and carbon reporting, we’re turning observability data into actionable insights for sustainable computing.You’ll see how real HEP workloads use this framework to visualize performance, uncover inefficiencies, and guide smarter infrastructure decisions. The goal: empower every team to understand the environmental footprint of their cloud-native applications—and make sustainability a first-class metric in our stacks.

events.title: To Make an SLO, First You Must Invent the Universe: OpenTelemetry Collection From Scratch - Cail Young, Octopus Deploy
events.description: In 2025, a team at Octopus Deploy was tasked with building out a multi-region, highly-available, autoscaling OpenTelemetry-based telemetry processing platform. We'd never had something like this before, and didn't have many documented examples to work from.This case study aims to make public the moving parts required to deliver a brand new telemetry pipeline based on OpenTelemetry Collector and Kubernetes, and will examine the many tradeoffs and misadventures we went on while building it out.

events.title: Observability in the Matrix: Choosing the Right AI Agent To Escape the Chaos - Henrik Rexed, Dynatrace
events.description: In a world flooded with metrics, logs, and traces does AI-driven observability help us escape the chaos, or does it simply build a more sophisticated illusion? You wake up in a Kubernetes cluster that seems fine until latency spikes, noisy logs, and missing traces reveal the truth: you're in the Observability Matrix. In this talk, we’ll guide you through five AI-powered agents: HolmesGPT, Kagent, Opni, K8sGPT, and Monte Carlo; each offering a different pill to help you escape the chaos. Like Neo choosing between red and blue, you’ll learn how these tools uncover root causes, enrich signals, reduce alert fatigue, and trace the behavior of autonomous agents. But beware: not all AI is your ally. We’ll explore the promises and pitfalls of AI-driven observability, and how to avoid being trapped in a false sense of insight. Join us for a journey through the Matrix of metrics, logs, and traces—and leave with the knowledge to choose your observability destiny.

events.title: Observability Through Users' Eyes and How To Fix the Knowledge Disconnect - Iris Dyrmishi, Miro & Hélia Barroso, Five9
events.description: Logs, metrics and traces nowadays are common words in every engineer's vocabulary, as Observability is everywhere with the rise of OpenTelemetry and improved standards. Instrumentation? No problem. Getting data from any system? Done. But then soon after, things start to fall apart, telemetry piles up unused, cloud bills soar, dashboards rot and alerts confuse even seasoned engineers. Clearly there is something missing.In this talk, Iris and Hélia share some experiences from building Observability platforms, patterns they have discovered of how users interact with telemetry data. From common questions across different teams and companies, pitfalls and also, strategies that empower teams to move from confusion to confidence.So get practical insights to bridge the gap between Observability data and User Knowledge, whether you're building platforms, supporting teams, or trying to make sense of your own telemetry chaos.

events.title: From Pods To PUE: A Digital Twin Approach To Sustainable Kubernetes Infrastructure - Matteo Bunino, CERN
events.description: As data centers face surging AI and scientific workloads and tighter sustainability targets, even small gains in energy efficiency matter. This talk presents a Kubernetes-native approach to building a digital twin for data center energy optimization. Combining telemetry from Kepler/eBPF with the new Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) API, we enable dynamic throttling and resizing of Intel and NVIDIA devices via RAPL and NVML/DCGM interfaces. AI models profile scientific workloads to infer resource intensity and criticality, letting the scheduler adapt allocations in real time to cluster utilization and cooling conditions. The twin predicts the impact of scheduling decisions on Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE), stabilizing node utilization and preventing cooling or PSU spikes. This work envisions a feedback loop where Kubernetes and digital-twin models cooperate to orchestrate sustainable, energy-aware data centers.

events.title: Parity in Observability Across Different Platforms - Kirill Mikhailov & Liam Harrington, Riot Games
events.description: Riot Games is the leading video game company with millions of players around the world.Strong observability is essential to ensuring that we deliver the best possible experience to the players. Which amplified by the fact that most of our games are competitive online games where every latency spike may ruin the experience.Because we operate our games at such scale - across multiple countries and regions, we’ve discovered that we cannot just use a single observability platform due to technical and other limitations. And as a result, we use multiple observability solutions across our ecosystem, for some of them we do not have a direct access.This introduce a new challenge: how do we maintain parity between different platforms?This presentation is focused on how we navigate parity between observability platforms, and how we utilise AI and classic approaches to that.

events.title: CANCELLED: Designing for Observability - George Hantzaras, MongoDB
events.description: Observability is a key aspect of modern software design, allowing teams to monitor and understand their systems in real-time. But designing for observability can be challenging, with a wide range of tools and best practices to consider.In this presentation, we'll cover strategies for designing systems that are observable, meaning that they can be monitored and understood in real-time. We'll explore the best practices and tools for designing systems that are observable, with a focus on practical tips and techniques that teams can implement in their own projects. We'll also dive into topics such as instrumentation, data collection, and analysis, as well as emerging trends in observability tools and approaches. Whether you're a software engineer, architect, or DevOps professional, this presentation will provide valuable insights and practical takeaways on how a systems design and architecture can play a role in the system's observability and how to implement best practices.

events.title: One YAML To Rule Them All: Declarative Config - Jamie Danielson, Honeycomb.io
events.description: When I joined my current company, I had to learn to write and maintain code in multiple languages. I didn’t need to be an expert, but I needed to contribute meaningfully beyond JavaScript. Many engineers face this same challenge where they must occasionally act as a polyglot programmer. Then they’re told to implement OpenTelemetry, dealing with each language’s setup, idioms, and quirks.We needed a language agnostic solution. Environment variables helped a bit, but they weren’t enough for more complex configurations. Setting an OTLP endpoint is one thing; adjusting instrumentation preferences is another. Declarative configuration, which uses YAML, was created to bridge this gap. After several years of development, it’s regaining momentum as the schema stabilizes and various languages add support. This talk will give a brief overview of declarative configuration, demo how it can be used in a polyglot application, and share the latest progress and what’s next for the project.

events.title: Let Me Be Your OpenTelemetry Champ - Pavol Loffay, Red Hat & Nicolas Wörner, OllyGarden
events.description: Managing a production OpenTelemetry deployment is a major operational challenge. With so many independent components, handling breaking changes, tuning sampling, and keeping pipelines healthy feels like a constant battle, leading to a fragmented experience. What if you had an expert to manage it for you?This talk introduces a powerful OpenTelemetry MCP Server that acts as your "OTel Champ." It enables an AI agent to intelligently manage your entire OpenTelemetry stack. This server profiles incoming telemetry, understands breaking changes, and automatically configures collectors and instrumentation based on real-time data. We will demonstrate how this AI-powered "Champ" automates complex tasks, allowing you to achieve automated cost optimization, dynamic PII filtering, and hands-free collector configuration.

events.title: OpenTelemetry Gateways: Enforce, Transform, Route - Juraci Paixão Kröhling, OllyGarden & Natalie Ujuk, IG Group
events.description: Teaching every development team about telemetry best practices doesn't scale. When bad data flows through pipelines (PII leakage, misconfigured attributes, cost-inflating noise) you need a centralized control point. OpenTelemetry collector gateways provide that enforcement layer between applications and backends.This talk presents proven gateway architectures from production environments managing cloud and on-premises telemetry. We'll demonstrate validation rules enforcing semantic conventions, transformation pipelines that enrich or redact data, and routing configurations enabling backend flexibility without developer refactoring. You'll see real configurations: blocking PII, enforcing mandatory attributes, and filtering high-cardinality data before it leaves your premises.We'll cover performance at scale, security boundaries for multi-tenant environments, and managing configuration drift. Attendees leave with blueprints and configurations ready for deployment.

events.title: Observability Without Borders: The OpenTelemetry Collector in a WebAssembly World - Evan Bradley, Dynatrace & Pablo Baeyens, Datadog
events.description: From Kubernetes clusters to edge devices and serverless functions, the OpenTelemetry Collector has proven to be a versatile tool for managing telemetry data. But what if we could take it a step further?In this session, we’ll explore how compiling the OpenTelemetry Collector to WebAssembly (Wasm) unlocks new possibilities for observability in constrained or unconventional environments, such as browser runtimes, language runtimes, and other Wasm platforms. We’ll discuss the technical journey of adapting the Collector to run on Wasm, highlighting the challenges of managing dependencies, optimizing performance, and tailoring its functionality to fit these environments. Whether you’re an observability enthusiast, platform engineer, or developer working on edge or serverless applications, this talk will inspire you to rethink what’s possible with OpenTelemetry in the Wasm era.

events.title: When the Codebase Starts Instrumenting Itself - Whitney Lee, Datadog
events.description: Commit Story is a GenAI powered, automated engineering journal that is triggered by Git commits. But that's not the point. For the speaker, this personal project became a playground for exploring how telemetry can inform AI coding assistants at development time.Her path to adding instrumentation likely mirrors that of many teams: an effort to follow OpenTelemetry semantic conventions, reinforced first through documentation, then later through a shared library of standards. Eventually, she built an AI agent that reads OpenTelemetry docs, discovers conventions, extends the standards module, instruments the code, and validates that it all works by querying the backend.This talk shares what that process revealed—how automation reshapes telemetry design—and includes a live demo showing how integrating OpenTelemetry Weaver formalized those standards, enabling the Telemetry Agent to work across multiple codebases.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: A Drop-in System To Accelerate Metrics Observability by 100x Using Sketch-based Approximation - Milind Srivastava, Carnegie Mellon University
events.description: Metrics observability workloads are growing in scale, resulting in (a) higher cost to operate observability infrastructure, and (b) slower query latencies.The usual approaches to deal with these are:- sample data- roll up data- reduce data cardinality- send less queriesAll of these approaches compromise the coverage of the observability infrastructure and can result in missing important anomalous behavior.Through our research, we have developed a radically new approach to achieve large scale, low cost, and low latency without compromising the coverage of the observability infrastructure.Our system reduce querying cost and latency by 100x by using 2 key techniques:- streaming precomputation- sketch-based approximationOur system is developed as a drop-in accelerator to an existing Prometheus-Grafana stack i.e. we require no changes to or replacement of Prometheus or Grafana.We will release an open-source prototype around the end of 2025.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Semantics Matter! Lessons From Stabilizing OpenTelemetry’s System Conventions - Roger Coll, Elastic
events.description: After more than two years of collaboration, the OpenTelemetry System Semantic Conventions group is nearing its first stabilization release. System metrics form the foundation of many observability agents, and defining a consistent, vendor-neutral, and future-proof naming model proved both complex and essential. This lightning talk will share key lessons from the journey toward consensus — the debates, trade-offs, and breakthroughs that shaped the conventions. We’ll also explore how semantic conventions directly influence OpenTelemetry tooling (like the Collector) and bring tangible value to users — enabling meaningful alerts, insightful dashboards, and efficient data organization across observability platforms.

How they realized their k8s priority constructs had become incorrect over time, and how bad the situation was.2. How they went about fixing it - by gauging the real priority of applications by utilizing only a wide set of metrics, interaction patterns, and, some clever thinking!!

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: “Metrics That Lie”: Understanding OpenTelemetry’s Cardinality Capping and Its Implications - Cijo Thomas, Microsoft
events.description: High-cardinality metrics just got safer with OpenTelemetry’s cardinality capping—SDKs won’t get OOM-killed anymore—but there’s a dangerous catch. When cardinality limits are hit, new attribute sets are folded into a special overflow attribute. The problem? Metric backends like Prometheus don’t treat this overflow attribute as special—it’s just another label—so queries can silently return incorrect results.Imagine querying error counts for /endpoint1. Once the cap is hit, new errors for that endpoint get folded into overflow. A query like errors WHERE url="/endpoint1" might underreport errors—masking real issues.This lightning talk explains how OTel’s cardinality capping works, how it can create hidden observability traps, how to detect when your queries may be incorrect, and strategies to avoid falling into this subtle but impactful pitfall.The spec for cardinality capping is already stable, and support is rolling out across SDKs—now’s the time to understand its impact and prepare.

events.title: From Mild To Wild: How Hot Can Your SLSA Be? - Andrew McNamara, Red Hat & Adolfo García Veytia, Carabiner Systems
events.description: Policy engines can consume attestations at three heat levels. Start mild and turn up the heat as you mature.Mild: Verify builder identity, SLSA levels, signatures, attestations. Answer "Who built this?" and "Does it meet standards?"Medium: Generate Verification Summary Attestations from multiple sources. Deploy admission controllers blocking nonconforming workloads from deploying. Enforce standards automatically. Attestations drive decisions, not documentation.Wild: Build trust chains through provenance. Chain attestations across the software lifecycle, from the build environment, down to the consumer. Enabling verifiers to reason about artifacts, not just their signatures. Demands process maturity but unlocks flexible security.We demonstrate implementations at each level using policy engines, including AMPEL and Conforma. Discover which heat level matches your organization and how to turn up the temperature.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: A Case Study in Cross-Ecosystem Security Response - Lori Lorusso, Rust Foundation
events.description: Companies use multiple open source projects and programming languages in their product stack creating a potential ‘wheel of misfortune’ when thinking about the rise in software supply chain attacks. Taking it one step further malicious actors are targeting programming languages to get to the core of how products are built. The rise in attacks at the programming language level seen as recently as September 2025 has amplified the need for cross communication between language ecosystems. Using the phishing attacks on Rust and Python as a case study we will show how Alpha-Omega facilitated stronger links between ecosystems, specifically the Rust Foundation and Python Software Foundation (grantees of Alpha-Omega) and how this helped improve security response times and outcomes when the phishing attacks were launched.

events.description: Before we had "observability" or "cloud native," we had packets. Gerald Combs, creator of Wireshark, reflects on nearly three decades of maintaining one of the world’s most ubiquitous open-source tools. In this keynote, Gerald shares the truth about building a community that lasts: why visibility is still the cornerstone of security, and how the lessons of the past are the only way to secure the complex, ephemeral future of Kubernetes.

events.title: Trust, Tampering, and Transparency: What History Can Teach Us About Open Source Security - Lisa Tagliaferri, Chainguard
events.description: From encrypted letters and palimpsests to reproducible builds and digital attestations, humans have long wrestled with how to prove authenticity, preserve integrity, and recover from tampering. As a medievalist turned software security practitioner, I explore what centuries of information protection can teach us about today’s open source supply chain. This talk connects historical practices of trust and preservation to modern supply chain security. Using examples from early cryptography and material reuse, we’ll examine frameworks like Sigstore, SLSA, Wolfi, in-toto, Tekton, SBOMs, and GUAC, showing how they extend timeless concepts of verification and transparency into digital systems. By pairing the cultural lineage of security with today’s open tooling, attendees will gain a deeper understanding of both the technical and human dimensions of trust.

events.description: In this talk, Georg and Jan argue that software vendors with a strong upstream engagement will have a significantly easier time meeting EU Cyber Resilience Act (CRA) obligations due to their network of developers across upstream projects, due to a better insight into projects health, and due to their generally better capability to eliminate vulnerabilities in their upstream dependencies.Georg will show how an OSPO can lead the necessary cultural change within a company and rally industry partners in the OpenSSF around the common cause of improving the upstream security posture. Jan will share how his team has successfully contributed security fixes to more than half of the CNCF projects. In combination, this talk and the examples presented make a strong case for increased upstream engagement by software vendors, thereby directly benefiting and strengthening the ecosystem.

events.description: At KubeCon EU 2023 in Amsterdam ING revealed its "Zero Privilege Architecture" on which its on premise ING Container Hosting Platform ("ICHP") is based.In this Talk we'll do a short recap of that architecture model and analyze some of the major security events of the past 3 years and how these did (not) impact us.This by explaining which aspects/controls of this architecture prevented or mitigated the threat(s) at hand.We hope to inspire the audience to think beyond the traditional security paradigm (shipping/installing insecure software, overprovisioning of privileges, dealing with false positives, praying for a patch for detected vulnerabilities to appear soon and work like a maniac to try to get all available patches applied timely...., ) and show there is a feasible alternative (and yes, that does takes effort to achieve...).And we'll give some more detailed insights in how "Zero Privilege Architecture" actually works under the hood in ING.This talk is 100% AI free ;-)

events.title: Quantum Proofing Sigstore: A Tale of Three Approaches - Kevin Conner & Firas Ghanmi, Red Hat
events.description: Implementing post quantum cryptography in supply chain security requires decisions beyond algorithm selection, with trade offs impacting performance and storage. This talk explores three approaches for adding PQC into Sigstore, the open standard for signing and verifying artifacts. The first implements hybrid X.509 certificates with dual classical/PQC signatures, modifying signing, verification and transparency components. The second maintains classical certificates, adding PQC signatures to transparency log inclusion proofs. The third leverages merkle tree certificates, embedding PQC commitments for efficient batch verification with minimal overhead. The talk presents prototypes, demonstrates post quantum signing and verification, reveals compromises in each approach and how assumptions in existing code created unexpected challenges. Attendees gain understanding of each method, providing a foundation for contributing to community discussions around post quantum adoption.

events.title: Tarmageddon: One Bug, Four Forks, and a Disclosure Scavenger Hunt - Marina Moore & Alex Zenla, Edera
events.description: You’ve heard of unmaintained software libraries, but what about libraries maintained in four places? What happens when several people create popular forks of a project and then a vulnerability impacts all of them? When we found a parsing vulnerability in tokio-tar, dubbed Tarmageddon, we discovered what happens next. We’ll walk through this vulnerability and how the responsible disclosure ended up sent to four different projects. We’ll look at how this disclosure became a scavenger hunt for maintainer email addresses, popular forks, and dependent projects.We’ll then zoom out to the ecosystem that made this disclosure so challenging. Why does rust have separate libraries for asynchronous code? Why do developers make a new library instead of adding features to the ones we already have? As a community, what can we do to make this better? We’ll discuss the roles of open source maintainers, foundations, and community members in making our whole ecosystem more secure.

events.title: Secure Your MCP Servers With OAuth, JWT, SPIFFE and More - Lin Sun, Solo.io & Yi Yang, IBM
events.description: You have built your first MCP server and tested it with the MCP Inspector, but it currently runs over stdio or streamable HTTP without HTTPS. Now you are asking yourself: should you rewrite your server to add authentication and authorization, or is there a smarter approach? And what happens when you scale to multiple MCP servers? Do you really need to modify each one?In this demo-driven session, Lin first shows the hard way: modifying MCP server code to add OAuth support, then rebuilding and redeploying. After that, she introduces a simpler method that offloads authentication and authorization through an MCP gateway, which allows you to secure your servers using OAuth, JWT, SPIFFE and other identity systems without changing or redeploying them. Finally, she shows how her AI agent interacts with the MCP server tools securely, with proper authentication and access control.

events.title: Simplifying Global Compliance for CNCF Projects With the OpenSSF OSPS Baseline - Madalin Neag, OpenSSF
events.description: Open source security is facing an increasingly complex global regulatory landscape. The Open Source Project Security (OSPS) Baseline, released in February 2025 with partners including CNCF and FINOS, lays out 40 practical requirements across three maturity levels, covering areas like access control, build and release, governance, documentation, and vulnerability management.What makes it powerful is how it maps directly to major frameworks, including the U.S. NIST SSDF and the EU’s NIS2, DORA, and CRA.This session looks at how the Baseline (and its latest evolutions), together with the OpenSSF Global Cyber Policy Working Group, helps navigate this diverse policy landscape. It shows how the Baseline is evolving to connect developer security practices with international regulations, supporting compliance across multiple frameworks, and offering a reliable, evidence-based foundation to build trust across the global open source ecosystem.

events.title: Thinking About Source Code Security in New Ways - Yongjae Chung & Justin Cappos, New York University
events.description: Code lives in repositories, and we rely on forges like Github or Gitlab to host them. But in the growing age of software supply chain attacks, it is becoming more necessary to have additional security in our own hands.We would like to introduce gittuf, a Git security system that brings security to the client side. With gittuf, every developer can independently verify source code security policies, giving them another layer of resilience against attacks.With gittuf, a repository can define its own policies and delegate authorization to maintainers, who can also declare their own policies within their delegated namespaces. This provides a granular, distributed trust model. gittuf can be used in tandem with existing forge workflows, providing a smooth transition to a hardened repository for your projects.gittuf is an incubating project at the Open Source Security Foundation, and currently in collaboration with multiple projects that are working to meet SLSA source track requirements.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Your Most Privileged User Isn't Human: The Agent Security Blindspot - Atulpriya Sharma, Improving
events.description: Your security team locked down Kubernetes. Then developers deployed AI agents needing kubectl, cloud APIs, observability access. You created service accounts with broad permissions.Now your most privileged identity makes decisions you can't audit, executes actions you can't attribute, invokes tools you can't scope.Traditional security assumes predictable workloads. Agents are autonomous - your controls don't apply.Three security gaps:- Lost Attribution: Audit logs show service account, not which user or why. No accountability.- Permission Escalation: Agents need broad capability, so they run elevated. Static RBAC can't express context-aware authorization.- Invisible Tool Chains: Agents invoke MCP servers dynamically. Security sees one identity; attackers see an attack path.As agents move to production, these become active vulnerabilities.Learn what to add to security reviews before this becomes your incident.

events.description: Supply Chain Software Security is a hot-button topic in the security and compliance domain of the open source software world. Specifically for end-user companies that are large, risk-averse, and depend on open source heavily. OSS projects, and the communities that surround them, are now compelled to adopt security best practices in order to position these projects as viable ones for commercial adoption.OpenSearch is a perfect example of a popular open source project, backed by heavyweights, and in use by a large number of companies. Therefore, securing this project is of paramount importance for the community.In this talk I intend to walk users through some security basics, while showcasing how to adopt them for OpenSearch. Some of the information will include scanning the repository, container images, dependency management, and deploying attestations, among others.

events.title: EU Cloud Sovereignty Framework Explained - Emiel Brok, SUSE / DOSBA
events.description: The EC Cloud Sovereignty Framework (v1.2.1, Oct 2025) is the definitive blueprint for EU digital autonomy. Born from Gaia-X and CIGREF, it moves beyond standard security to address critical dependencies on non-EU actors and proprietary lock-in.In this session, Emiel decodes the framework’s dual assessment approach: the Sovereignty Effective Assurance Level (SEAL) and the quantitative Sovereignty Score. We will break down the 8 Sovereignty Objectives (SOVs)—covering Legal, Data/AI, Supply Chain, and Technology—and map the journey through the 5 SEAL levels toward Full Digital Sovereignty (SEAL-4).Crucially, we will demonstrate why true sovereignty is impossible without open source. Attendees will learn how OSS directly enables the highest SEAL ratings and how "EU First" policies empower organizations to reclaim strategic control. This talk provides a clear roadmap for anyone navigating the future of sovereign digital procurement in Europe.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Progress Over Purity: Sovereignty That Actually Ships - Antoine Tielbeke, Team Rockstars IT
events.description: My European server runs on Intel/AMD CPUs. Is that still "sovereign"?This lightning talk challenges the all-or-nothing mindset that paralyzes sovereignty initiatives. Building infrastructure for Volt Europa, a pan-European political party with 35,000 members, taught me that meaningful progress beats ideological purity.The journey included uncomfortable discoveries: perfect sovereignty is impossible, European providers have their own problems (our OVH cluster got billed in Polish Zloty with no easy way to migrate), and waiting for perfection means never starting. So we prioritized by impact, migrating transactional email before marketing because it was critical and achievable.Three lessons in ten minutes: why portability matters more than perfection, how to prioritize migrations pragmatically, and why starting now beats waiting for the perfect provider.Stop demanding purity. Start making progress.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Designing for Exit: Why Portability Is a Sovereignty Requirement - Alireza Rahmani, Red Hat
events.description: Portability is often treated as a secondary concern, yet in sovereign cloud architectures it is a foundational requirement. Without a clear exit strategy, organizations risk losing control over both operations and future architectural decisions.This lightning talk focuses on designing for exit as a first-class architectural principle. It presents cloud-native patterns that support workload portability using open standards and Kubernetes-based platforms. The discussion connects design choices around packaging, deployment, and dependencies to their long-term impact on flexibility and control.The session emphasizes portability as a practical expression of technology sovereignty and shows how early architectural decisions determine an organization’s ability to evolve, migrate, or change platforms over time.

events.title: Running Sovereign Cloud Stack Cluster for International Telecommunication Union: Lessons Learned - Martin Pilka, dNation & Karsten Samaschke, VanillaCore GmbH
events.description: Many organizations today want to run their own private clusters which are fully open source, to ensure information security and avoid vendor lock-in. However, installing and operating such cluster is a nontrivial task.Sovereign Cloud Stack (SCS,https://sovereigncloudstack.org) is the European open source project that develops standards for sovereign cloud infrastructures.In this talk we describe how we installed SCS based cluster for International Telecommunication Union, what technical challenges we faced during the installation and how we successfully solved them, delivering cluster in record 2 weeks time.First part of talk focuses on SCS architecture and its IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) and KaaS (Kubernetes as a Service) layers. We describe components of cluster solution (e.g. control plane, worker plane, networking, storage, monitoring) and their interactions.In second part of talk we describe day 2 operations, technical challenges we faced and how we solved them.

events.title: Sovereignty and Open Source? How To Put Your Money Where Your Mouth Is! - Vasu Chandrasekhara, Independent & Christian Hüning, BWI GmbH
events.description: Historically, the military enforced standardization by leveraging its massive purchasing power to mandate its supply chain to produce equipment with interchangeable parts and specifications.Today, software has permeated into every (civil or military) crucial system, but interoperability remains an unsolved challenge in the high-stake goal for Software Defined Defense. With the relentless pace of innovation in software and current geopolitical urgency, the luxury of waiting for negotiated interoperability between vendors is gone. We must shift to dynamic, modular frameworks, which can deliver immediate, versatile, field-ready integrations.We present the Germany Army’s strategy for an Open Defense Cloud that builds on multiple vendors, all collaborating in LF, CNCF, and NeoNephos’ neutral OSS projects. We derive the ecosystem strategy leveraging the inherent motivation of vendors to use OSS.The OSS ecosystem thinking tied with purchasing power is pivotal for digital sovereignty.

events.title: Bridging CNCF and OpenInfra: Kubernetes as the Unified Control Plane for Sovereign Clouds - Michael Schmidt, SAP SE
events.description: Europe’s cloud infrastructure landscape is fragmented making interoperable and sovereign cloud platforms hard to build. This session presents a technical deep dive into CobaltCore, a Kubernetes enabled OpenStack distribution that bridges the CNCF and OpenInfra ecosystems.By combining Kubernetes’ declarative control model with mature Infrastructure as a Service capabilities, CobaltCore supports workloads that cannot easily be replatformed to fully cloud-native designs. A containerized OpenStack managed by Kubernetes and the Kubernetes Resource Model enables a fully automated lifecycle from bare metal provisioning to workload delivery.The session examines using key projects of the Apeiro Reference Architecture, the technical blueprint of the IPCEI-CIS initiative, for bare metal automation, vendor agnostic networking, large scale Kubernetes operations, security and compliance management, and signed software delivery with end to end provenance.

events.title: Beyond the DBaaS Trap: Achieving Data Sovereignty With Kubernetes and CloudnativePG - Floor Drees & Gabriele Bartolini, EDB
events.description: Digital sovereignty requires "operational sovereignty", that is, the ability to run critical workloads on any infrastructure without proprietary cloud API limits.As global regulations like the EU Data Act set new standards for data portability, organisations must prioritise architectures that ensure long-term independence.Managed databases (DBaaS) are the primary point of lock-in, creating a "gravity" that prevents migration to sovereign clouds.Gabriele and Floor demonstrate how to break this cycle using Kubernetes and CloudNativePG to build a cloud-neutral PostgreSQL architecture that satisfies modern sovereignty requirements.Attendees will explore how to meet business continuity requirements that remain identical across hyperscalers and bare metal, while learning practical strategies for data-level portability. This approach ensures an organisation can move its most critical asset to stay ahead of regulatory changes without sacrificing the developer experience.

events.description: In an era of increasing geopolitical tension and systemic cyber threats, the "kill switch"- the ability of a single provider or foreign entity to remotely disable critical digital services - has moved from a theoretical risk to a present danger.This panel brings together a unique group of stakeholders to dissect the real-world impact of digital dependency and possible solutions. We will hear the first-hand account of a former Town Mayor whose municipality suffered a major cyber attack, exposing the fragility of non-sovereign systems. Experts from the Sovereign Cloud Stack (SCS) and the NeoNephos Foundation will join leaders from the Open Source industry to discuss the roadmap for a solution of the current crisis and explore:*How dependency on non-sovereign vendors amplifies cyber, legal, and operational risks*How open source and sovereign cloud initiatives reduce systemic risk and strengthen resilience*What municipalities and institutions can do to move away from closed systems

events.title: Organising Your Code and State for Governance and Self-Service - John Keates, Wehkamp Retail Group
events.description: As your infrastructure and the code that defines it grows, you'll eventually end up with a seemingly unwieldy amount of dependencies in a variety of files and modules.While you can manipulate the state to move and sort your way out of such a mess with splitting and moving state around, moving to a more constructive separation of concerns allowed us stop the growth of our mess and come up with a much more productive design to migrate to.When we were working on this, we didn't really know what we were going to end up with or what would suit us, so instead of coming up with a solution right away, we somewhat accidentally came up with a framework instead. While the way we're currently using is by no means the biggest scale this framework could support, it gave us some much needed room for growth in the amount of providers, environments and states we need to use to create robust, repeatable infrastructure that allows for strong governance and self-service for everyone involved.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Staying in Sync: Managing Upgrades in OpenTofu and Terraform Modules - Fabrizio Sgura, Veritas Automata
events.description: As known, OpenTofu is an open-source fork of Terraform that maintains backward compatibility, and in this lightning talk I want to focus on covering best practices for upgrading OpenTofu itself and keeping Terraform modules up-to-date. I discuss version pinning, semantic versioning of modules, and automated upgrade tools.For example, I talk about running openTofu init and openTofu apply across version changes, showing how state files migrate. I also recommend tools like terraform-provider-migrate or CI bots to handle common pitfalls. The goal is a fast overview of how to safely evolve infrastructure-as-code configurations so they stay in sync with CNCF’s OpenTofu releases.

events.title: Debugging Slow Infrastructure Runs With OpenTelemetry in OpenTofu - James Humphries, Spacelift / OpenTofu
events.description: Infrastructure-as-code deployments often suffer from performance issues, with runs taking minutes or hours without visibility into bottlenecks. In GitOps environments, this directly impacts deployment velocity, but traditional logging provides insufficient insight into what's actually slow.This talk demonstrates how OpenTofu's native OpenTelemetry integration brings cloud-native observability to infrastructure deployments. I'll talk through real-world scenarios where OTel traces exposed hidden bottlenecks—slow provider downloads, inefficient state operations, and API throttling, all of which are situations that may be invisible in traditional logs.

events.title: Highly Illogical: Safely Leverage Logic in TF - Robert Glenn, Kopacetic, Inc.
events.description: Logical operations in OpenTofu/Terraform (TF) resemble a functional programming style, which is esoteric and can be overly convoluted. But the vast majority of engineers who find themselves writing TF modules are often much more comfortable with an object oriented (Java, JavaScript), procedural (C), or scripted (Bash/PowerShell) paradigm.A common approach is to actually retreat: remove logic from the TF code, and implement it in other operations (leveraging the paradigm of choice). This leads to further problems as logic is peppered throughout pipeline code and Dockerfiles, or implemented by a web of manual procedures.In this talk, we disentangle ourselves from nested and inline TF functions, bringing structure and clarity to even the most complex flow of operations. We will also examine logic that may happen outside of the TF execution (either as an automatic or manual action) as orchestration processes, and present a mental model for determining where logic should reside.

events.title: Testing Infrastructure as Code Using OpenTofu and Localstack - Pieter Vincken, Cegeka
events.description: This session explores how standard development and testing best practices can be applied to infrastructure engineering. We’ll demonstrate how to write effective tests for IaC using OpenTofu, and how Localstack can dramatically shorten feedback loops by simulating AWS services locally. Attendees will learn practical techniques to validate infrastructure changes before they reach production, reducing risk and improving confidence in deployments.Key takeaways:- Why testing IaC is critical for reliability and agility.- How to apply proven software testing principles to infrastructure code.- Hands-on examples of testing OpenTofu configurations.- Leveraging Localstack for fast, local feedback without the need for an AWS environmentIf you manage infrastructure or develop IaC, this talk will equip you with actionable strategies to make testing a first-class citizen in your workflow.

events.description: When an internal audit revealed 800+ Datadog dashboards with zero version control, we had one month to move everything to OpenTofu without breaking security-critical monitoring.As the SRE leading this effort, here's what I learned:The Mission: Manage 800+ dashboards, monitors, and synthetic tests from ClickOps chaos to OpenTofu-managed infrastructure while maintaining zero downtime.The Approach: We built a custom framework tooling to automate 100% of the migration using Datadog API + OpenTofu import workflows with shadow-mode testing, preventing production disasters.

events.description: Testing is an essential part of software development. OpenTofu providers are no exception. However, testing OpenTofu providers comes with its own set of challenges. They will explore how engineers with varied experiences influence design, operation and scaling, highlighting areas like abstraction boundaries, self-service workflows, API touchpoints, onboarding paths and documentation.Attendees will discover practical strategies to improve adoption and maintainability by refining decision-making processes, optimizing feedback loops, and encouraging greater team participation within CNCF technical groups. By integrating diverse perspectives, platforms become easier to use, scalable, and adaptable, ultimately contributing to the overall sustainability of the ecosystem.

events.title: Platform Engineering Day l Sponsored Keynote: Why Kubernetes Node OS Still Shapes Performance, Security, and Scale - Naadir Jeewa, Broadcom
events.description: Containers abstract applications, but the Kubernetes node OS still plays a critical role. Kernel behavior, update strategies, and OS lifecycle alignment influence cluster stability, security posture, and upgrade confidence. This talk explores why platform engineers should treat the node OS as part of the Kubernetes platform contract, and how thoughtful OS choices help teams operate reliable, scalable clusters across diverse environments.

events.title: Platform Engineering Day | Sponsored Keynote: Bridging the Local Kubernetes Gap for AI Developers - Saiyam Pathak, vCluster
events.description: Everyone is building platforms. Increasingly, AI platforms with GPU scheduling, multi-tenancy, inference autoscaling.But the developer experience starts long before production. It starts locally — testing models, iterating on configs getting things working on a cluster for the first time.Today, there's a gap between that local experience and what actually runs in production. Local clusters are great for getting started, but when you need GPU access, environment sharing, or anything that resembles your real platform, you're on your own. Developers end up jumping straight to cloud environments, losing the fast feedback loop.In this keynote, I'll talk about closing that gap with vind (vCluster in Docker),  local Kubernetes that goes further. attach real GPU nodes over VPN, pause and resume clusters, share environments with your team. I'll show what makes it different from existing local cluster tools and how it gives AI developers the fast feedback loop they've been missing.

Based on emerging trends in the enterprise, this lightning talk explores how platform teams are evolving from infrastructure stewards into architects of centralized AI hubs.

events.title: Creating an IDP for AI Engineering - Max Körbächer, Liquid Reply GmbH
events.description: What couldn't be more hyped than AI? But where is such hype, some organizations struggle with the changes in their IT landscape and find new challenges in people, processes and the technology.Platform Engineering has proven its value in the past years, allowing organizations to scale almost unlimitedly while enabling product development flow.So, why don't we bring our decades of experience in automation, scaleability and reliability to AI? Well, it is already there. Every tool needed to train, deploy and use AI is cloud native. But it adds up to a completely new complexity on top of existing structures.That's where the AIDP comes in. Leveraging the known, integrating it with the existing solution, to provide a unified entry point for organizations ramping up their AI Engineering capabilities. Think of it like Backstage, just on steroids, platform and end user-friendly, with a dedication to be the one-stop shop for getting started with LLMs. It's the platform, not the model.
