events.title: Scaling on Satisfaction: Automated Rollouts Driven by User Feedback - Thomas Vitale, Systematic & Whitney Lee, Datadog
events.description: As the old saying goes, “the customer is always right”. When it comes to GenAI, the end users of our apps are indeed always right, because we can’t fully trust an LLM on its own. What if we used a new mechanism to guide the routing and rollout of new application versions? How about user feedback? Imagine that: the more an app variant gets up-voted, the more traffic is sent to it.In this session, Whitney and Thomas demo a platform that enables app developers to define success criteria for business operations involving GenAI, and to capture users’ feedback using OpenTelemetry - including how to correlate it with other observability data. Then comes the twist. They’ll expand the platform using Flagger and Knative so that the users control the rollout and routing of new apps. And today, YOU are the user, the audience!You’ll see how this technique can be applied beyond GenAI, and take part in an interactive story that evolves and changes course in real time based on your feedback!

events.description: Modern platform engineering is often described in terms of development, automation, and infrastructure - ultimately creating a golden path. What then about the people paving and walking that golden path? This talk explores what it truly means to “play support” for a platform team: enabling others to build, ship, and innovate faster without becoming a bottleneck. Playing support involves having the meetings no one else wants, creating the solutions no one can be asked to create. Drawing on lessons from large-scale platform initiatives at Bane NOR, we’ll discuss how internal platforms evolve from service providers to trusted partners by utilising their support. Attendees will gain practical insights on fostering alignment across platform teams, balancing autonomy with governance, and how this is achieved on a daily basis.

events.title: Promotion Failed: How (Not) To Stage Your (Kubernetes) Platform - Nicolai Ort, ODIT.Services
events.description: Creating a Kubernetes platform is one thing — getting teams to trust and adopt it is another.When it’s time for an upgrade, the margin for error suddenly feels razor-thin. A staging environment sounds like the obvious answer to prevent disruptions and keep your users' trust, but how do you make sure what passes staging truly reaches - and works - in production?In this talk, we’ll explore several strategies for building and promoting staged environments, from simple replication to declarative pipelines and automated diffing.Through the lens of a Kubernetes-as-a-Service platform, you’ll gain practical insight and tipps on designing a promotion process that fits your platform’s maturity and scale.

events.title: The Day 2 Hangover: What To Do After the Platform Party Ends - Cat Morris, Syntasso & Nitish Malhotra, Veeam Software
events.description: Often, new platform teams start building for day 1 concerns: new apps, experiments, and cool tech. Everyone is excited by shiny new self-service platforms. But success can be short-lived. When building platforms, it's easy to think of just creating things - a platform party! But as time goes by, it is hard to ignore the day 2 hangover.At Veeam, a global company known for its backup and recovery software, its platform-building efforts were so prolific that it had hundreds of components that app teams had to combine. The day 1 experience was amazing, but the day 2 concerns had been ignored. Operating, maintaining, and demonstrating the platform's value became increasingly challenging.In this talk, Nitish and Cat share how the team introduced platform orchestration patterns to simplify operations and reduce maintenance overhead. Without a complete rebuild, the team significantly reduced the cost to manage platform resources and freed up time to deliver real business value.

One year into Saxo Bank’s journey, we’re sharing the full story: what worked, what surprised us, and what we would do differently.In this talk, you’ll hear about:- Did things just work?- Was it possible to migrate large-scale workloads?- Did we actually manage to get 80% cost reduction?- Lessons learned around resilience, automation, and lifecycle management with kubeadm + Cluster API.Attendees will leave with a realistic understanding of the trade-offs, risks, and benefits of self-hosted Kubernetes at scale, plus actionable insights for planning and operating their own clusters.

You’ll leave inspired with ideas to boost adoption, reduce friction, and turn your platform into a collaborative, thriving ecosystem.

events.title: Overwhelmed by Scale: How Product Thinking Fixes Platform Teams - Dominik Schmidle, giantswarm.io
events.description: Your platform team started small. You automated Kubernetes cluster provisioning with some Terraform scripts, added useful services, teams adopted your platform. Success! Then reality hit: drowning in tickets, firefighting, endless upgrades. Your small team serves hundreds of developers with no time for strategic work. Leadership sees your platform as a cost center with unclear ROI.This pattern repeats everywhere. Platform teams get trapped building what's requested, reacting to fires, unable to say no or think strategically. The problem: treating platforms as service desks instead of products.Drawing from workshops with dozens of organizations, this talk shows how product thinking breaks the cycle. You'll learn to move from firefighting to strategic ownership through vision-driven roadmaps, prioritization enabling saying no, and value communication shifting platforms from cost centers to strategic assets, including practical patterns for restructuring teams.

events.description: Everyone talks about golden paths, but most teams struggle with: how do you actually build them?You've seen architecture diagrams, attended talks, but when you return to work, you're staring at scattered scripts, wondering where to start.This talk introduces the Maturity-First Construction Framework - a progressive methodology that draws from the Platform Maturity Model.Through a live demo, we'll focus on three critical transitions: evolving scripts into validation frameworks, implementing GitOps integration, and enabling self-service using ArgoCD and Backstage.You'll witness functional code being built, illustrating how teams can begin with simple scripts and systematically enhance their developer experience. We'll explore battle-tested patterns for validation, templating, and GitOps integration.You'll walk away with a proven construction playbook for building golden paths that scale with organizational maturity and deliver measurable developer productivity improvements.

events.title: How We Built an Internal Developer Platform and Evolved It Into Open Source - Gang Luo & Kristina Kondrashevich, Electrolux Group
events.description: At Electrolux Group, we operate in the IoT space, powering connected appliances across global markets. As our engineering organization grew from 10 to 300 developers with only 5 Ops engineers, we needed a scalable way to support delivery without slowing teams down.We first adopted SRE practices, but they proved difficult to scale across diverse product teams. Instead, we baked SRE principles directly into our platform. Using Backstage, OpenTofu, and custom platform APIs, we built an Internal Developer Platform (IDP) that automated infrastructure provisioning, standardized tools, and enabled self-service for developers.Over time, the IDP evolved into an open-source platform focused on cloud infrastructure management, making these capabilities accessible to others.In this talk, we’ll share our journey from SRE to Platform Engineering. We will cover the architecture, lessons learned, and how open-sourcing reshaped our approach to DevOps and developer productivity at scale.

events.description: Do you remember your first zero-day? How about the most recent? Who was involved in the response? What tools did they use? How long did it take to patch that CVE? Are you confident you didn’t miss anything?Most security teams are outnumbered by developers by factors of hundreds or even thousands to one. Many organisations don’t have a single security specialist, delegating that role to their infrastructure or platform teams.When asking these teams about how prepared they feel for the next supply chain attack or zero-day CVE the answer is always the same: “We’re f***ed”Our expert panel is here to help. Bringing deep expertise in security, platform engineering and cloud native we’re going to get practical and tactical about how to plan for the next zero-day.Because it’s not if, it’s when. We need to be ready.

events.title: Panel: Platform Resilience, Built on Belonging - Diana Todea, VictoriaMetrics; Stéphane Este-Gracias, ITQ; Or Navon, Friends of Dorothy; Milad Vafaeifard, DHHWG
events.description: Behind every high-impact Internal Developer Platform (IDP) lies a socio-technical system, but many platform teams struggle not with YAML or APIs but with attracting and retaining the right talent. A vast pool of skilled engineers hesitate to apply because they don't feel they belong, leaving organizations unknowingly missing out on innovation.This panel brings together members of Merge Forward - a CNCF initiative uniting community groups across disability, gender, neuro- and speech diversity - to explore how designing for belonging drives measurable business outcomes: stronger Developer Experience (DevEx), higher platform adoption, greater retention, and ROI.Through stories from platform engineers, attendees will learn how empathy, accessibility, and allyship accelerate team performance and maturity - turning cultural health into a core metric of business success.Because in platform engineering, resilience isn't just built into systems - it's built between people.

events.title: Backend-First IDP: A Production Roadmap With Argo CD, Crossplane, and OPA - Abhinav Sharma, KodeKloud & Aditya Soni, Sailpoint
events.description: The "portal-first" pattern cripples scale: teams build beautiful UIs (Backstage) over fragile, bespoke automation, leading to inevitable "portal fatigue." Our architectural lesson is definitive: backend-first wins.This session provides a production-validated blueprint for building a resilient IDP with orchestration and policy as the authoritative core. We prove the theory with a focused demo based on a multi-tenant, 100+ service environment.We share the operational blueprint (and anti-patterns) to:1. Showcasing our minimal Platform API/CRD contract that wires ArgoCD, Crossplane, and OPA2. Detail how policy is authored, bundled, and promoted to prevent composition drift and policy sprawl.3. Demonstrate the end-to-end golden pathThis approach cut infrastructure request lead time from days to minutes, eliminated portal-only snowflake workflows, and reduced GitOps drift incidents by 70%.

events.title: Building Extensible Platforms - Hazel Weakly, Nivenly Foundation
events.description: Have you ever tried to build a house on quicksand? I can’t imagine it would work very well! The ground is so chaotic and the landscape itself isn’t reliable enough to be the foundation you need. It feels like you can’t even get started, let alone build something sturdy enough to last.Honestly, trying to build a platform in today’s environment feels just as chaotic. Things are churning faster and faster, and just when you've finished implementing it, something's deprecated and broken. It feels like things get thrown out just as fast as they're built. Quarterly planning? Long-term thinking? Some days they feel like relics of the past, so how do we build platforms now?In this session, join Hazel as she walks through a journey of how she took a rapidly evolving environment, where a platform seemed impossible to build, and turned that chaos into something that long term success could be built on top of.

events.title: Istio in Production: Expected Value, Results, and Effort at GitHub Scale - Mitch Connors, Microsoft & Golnaz Rouhi, Github
events.description: GitHub has adopted Istio multi-cluster on tens of thousands of pods across 21 clusters to enforce mTLS and centralized authN/authZ for secure-by-default service communication. To scale mesh adoption, we built automation to onboard workloads without app changes and introduced custom ingress gateways to route external traffic. Istio’s traffic management enabled incremental canary rollouts: new versions are first exposed to internal staff, then gradually ramped to users with instant rollback on failure. In some cases, least-connection load balancing reduced latency by ~15 ms.Come learn how to migrate to Istio at GitHub scale, including the risks we mitigated (e.g., stale config, sidecar saturation, gateway load), and practical strategies for secure, scalable service mesh adoption. We’ll also discuss what’s next, including agentic traffic controls and Istio’s Ambient Mode.

events.title: The GitOps Paradox: Why Your Devs Need an API You Don't Want To Build - Simon Koudijs, ConfigButler
events.description: Platform teams face a paradox: developers need simple APIs and UIs, but building and maintaining them is a huge operational burden that pulls teams away from core platform work.This session coins "Reverse GitOps," an API-first pattern that solves this by placing a light Kubernetes control plane in front of your Git repository. We'll show how platform teams can define simple CRDs that act as a declarative API for their platform, which can then be used to build GUIs (Backstage/Headlamp) or to allow devs and AI agents to interact with the platform programmatically.When a user interacts with this API, the open-source `gitops-reverser` operator translates their high-level intent into a clean Git commit. This commit creates a pull request, preserving your existing review process. Once merged, this simple CR can be "exploded" into a full set of production-ready manifests by tools like KRO or Crossplane, integrating with your existing GitOps workflow powered by tools Flux or ArgoCD.

events.title: Scaling Platform-as-a-Product Without Losing Your Mind - Eline Henriksen, Telenor & Jakub Pawelczak, Google Cloud
events.description: As Telenor grew, we reached an inflection point. Growing our platform user base to hundreds of developers, platform development could no longer keep pace with our scale.To accelerate developer productivity and manage this growing complexity, we moved towards a Platform-as-a-Product (PaaP) approach—treating our internal platform as an intentional product with its own users, roadmap, and success metrics.In this session, Jakub from Google and Eline from Telenor will share real-world experiences building and scaling platform product teams to support hundreds of engineering teams.We’ll explore what it actually takes to make PaaP work at scale, including:- Building safety nets and paved paths that developers want to use- Driving ongoing education, enablement and collaboration between platform and product teams- Continuously gathering insights and feedback to evolve the platform based on real user needs- Establishing effective communication in large, distributed organizations

events.title: When Platform Engineers Lead FinOps: Driving Reliability and $20M in Savings - Aditya Sharma & Mohit Kumar, Expedia Group
events.description: Platform engineers have long been guardians of reliability — designing resilient control planes, enforcing SLAs, and keeping 1,000s of workloads alive. Yet cloud cost is often handed off to FinOps teams armed with scripts and spreadsheets, unaware of how platforms actually work. The result? “Savings” that trade reliability for cost.This talk is a call for Platform Engineers to adopt cost-conscious design, operations, and culture. As platform owners, we know how systems respond to changes, and where optimizations can be safely made without compromising SLAs.At Expedia Group, this mindset saved us $20M in 2025 while improving reliability across 500+ clusters and 10K+ workloads. We break down three key patterns:- Revisiting the Legacy- Establishing a View and Baselines- Embracing Re-architecture without FearAttendees will leave with a field-tested playbook to embed FinOps into their platforms (no prior FinOps knowledge required), and a sense of responsibility towards efficiency.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: No Portal? No Problem! How We Scaled Our Platform With Just Terraform and ArgoCD - Maryam Tavakkoli, RELEX Solutions
events.description: Many platform talks focus on shiny developer portals and custom APIs, yet many successful platforms scale without starting there. At RELEX Solutions, we built an internal developer platform powering multiple teams across Azure Kubernetes Service clusters without a portal. Using Terraform and ArgoCD as our foundation, we structured GitOps workflows that allowed teams to deploy infrastructure, add-ons, and applications independently while maintaining platform integrity. In this lightning talk, I’ll share how simplicity, clear repo structures, and GitOps processes empowered our developers, scaled our platform effectively, and solved real-world multi-team challenges, proving that sometimes, less is more.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Production-Grade AI Isolation: K0s + Kata Containers for Zero-Trust Infrastructure - Prashant Ramhit, Mirantis
events.description: AI platforms in regulated sectors like insurance demand not only performance and scalability, but governance, auditability, and trust built directly into the runtime.This session explores how k0s, a single-binary k8s distribution, combined with Kata Containers, provides a minimal yet powerful foundation for governed AI workloads.By embedding isolation, attestation, and policy enforcement at the cluster core, we demonstrate how governance is not an add-on, it's intrinsic.Each model or inference workload runs inside a micro-VM, ensuring data isolation and verifiable execution and aligning with emerging CNCF AI Conformance principles.Attendees will learn how to build reproducible, secure AI environments, from data center to edge. where governance starts at the infrastructure layer, not the application tier.Takeaway: A practical blueprint for secure, compliant, and observable AI in insurance.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: Lessons from Putting AI in Front of a Platform: Taming the Non-Deterministic Beast - Shane Dowling, Syntasso
events.description: Agentic AI systems can do anything — and that’s the problem.In this lightning talk, Shane Dowling shares lessons learned from putting AI in front of a real developer platform. You’ll hear what happens when an MCP Server gets too much context, why “more data” often means less control, and how introducing structure and opinionated boundaries can transform chaotic AI behaviour into something useful and reliable.Using experiments with Claude, FastMCP, and Kratix as a practical example, Shane explores how to design safer, more predictable agentic systems — and how to build interfaces where human and AI operators can actually collaborate productively.Attendees will leave with strategies for taming non-deterministic systems, avoiding common pitfalls, and designing the guardrails that let AI thrive safely in cloud-native environments.

events.title: OTel Me About It: Latest Updates in WASI OTel - Caleb Schoepp, Fermyon & Andrew Steurer, Independent
events.description: With WebAssembly firmly in production, it is more important than ever to have observable Wasm workloads. OpenTelemetry is the gold standard for modern observability and now it’s coming to WebAssembly. This talk presents the WASI OTel proposal, a new WASI interface that enables using OpenTelemetry SDKs from within your Wasm components. You’ll see how traces, metrics, and logs can be emitted seamlessly from Wasm applications, with a live demo using Spin, the leading framework for building and running serverless Wasm workloads. We’ll unpack the key design choices behind WASI OTel and show how the WebAssembly component model unlocks powerful new capabilities, such as automatically enriching traces with information from the component graph. We’ll close with an update on the proposal’s progress within both the WebAssembly and OpenTelemetry communities, and how this work is shaping the future of observability for Wasm in production.

events.title: Streamlining WebAssembly Builds with Bunny - Charalampos Mainas & Anastassios Nanos, Nubis PC
events.description: Building WebAssembly (Wasm) is challengingdue to fragmented ad-hoc scripts and custom toolchains that vary across languages, runtimes, and projects. This lack of cohesion increases maintenance overhead and makesreproducible builds difficult. Unlike container-based workflows, current Wasm build processes are inconsistent and manual, slowing adoption and complicating deployment across environments.Bunny is an open-source project that unifies how applications build against various frameworks and platforms, including Wasm.Using a simple declarative configuration, Bunny compiles source code to Wasm/WASI, creates components, and produces OCI-compatible artifacts ready for deployment on any runtime or orchestration platform.This session presents Bunny’s design and workflow, showing how a consistent build interface simplifies Wasm development across environments. A live demo will illustrate how source code becomes a ready-to-run Wasm module using a simple declarative build process.

events.title: Running Wasmtime in Hardware-Isolated Microenvironments - Danilo (Dan) Chiarlone, Microsoft
events.description: WebAssembly gives you a sandbox. VMs give you hardware isolation. Can you have both without destroying cold start times? For serverless workloads needing defense-in-depth, traditional VMs are too slow. The answer is yes, but only if you rethink what a VM needs to be.This talk shows how to integrate hardware isolation with Wasm runtimes using two approaches. Hyperlight-Wasm strips away the OS: no kernel, no system calls, just memory and a CPU running no-std Wasmtime. Guest code communicates through the WebAssembly Component Model and WIT. Hyperlight-Nanvix pairs Hyperlight with a lightweight microkernel that gives you syscalls with kernel-level filtering and Rust standard library support.You'll see architecture, code, and live demos for both. We'll cover how communication works with and without an OS, what breaks and works in each model, and performance tradeoffs. For anyone building platforms that run untrusted code, working on Wasm tooling, or curious about hardware isolation.

events.title: GraalVM: WebAssembly for the JVM Ecosystem - Fabio Niephaus & Shaun Smith, Oracle
events.description: The JVM is one of the world’s most widely used programming platforms, yet it’s rarely mentioned in conjunction with WebAssembly—until now! GraalVM brings Wasm to the JVM ecosystem with two projects: the GraalWasm runtime, and GraalVM Web Image, a new JVM bytecode-to-Wasm compiler.This session introduces GraalWasm, an embeddable Wasm runtime with excellent tooling and exceptional performance thanks to the optimizing Graal JIT compiler. We'll demo and debug a Rust library, compiled to Wasm, and embedded in a Spring Boot microservice.We’ll also present GraalVM Web Image which can compile JVM applications into efficient Wasm modules that start up fast and run in the browser, on mobile, and in the cloud. We’ll explain how it works, highlight practical use cases, demo end-to-end workflows, and discuss current limitations and what’s next.If you’re interested in bringing Wasm to the legions of independent and enterprise JVM developers who have yet to embrace it, this session is for you!

events.title: Split Rendering TV Applications Using WASM - Timothy Pearce & Rajiv Ramdhany, BBC
events.description: Public Service Broadcasters have a remit to provide universal access to their content. This poses a unique technical challenge when developing Video Streaming Applications which have to run on devices like Smart TVs, which often have constrained hardware. Additionally, audiences are expecting more from applications such as richer user interfaces with smooth animations and more interaction, which some devices struggle with.We have been investigating how to use WebAssembly to author flexible applications which can offload operations such as UI rendering and animation from client devices to edge compute and the cloud. Richer animations and interactions impose additional performance constraints. We have looked at whether offloading UI-rendering delivers the required quality of experience. Offloading rendering WASM modules is simplified if these are truly portable. We describe our approach to try to make UI-component rendering with WASM modules portable, highlight the challenges and describe the mechanisms to stream the rendered UIs at low-latency to the client.

events.title: ⚡Lightning Talk: WasmPulse: Quantifying Wasm Sustainability With WASI Preview 2 Performance Numbers - Marcin Ziółkowski, Orange
events.description: As WebAssembly adoption grows, it faces a critical performance data gap, lacking the mature monitoring tools and standardized metrics of the container ecosystem, especially regarding resource usage and sustainability. This talk introduces WasmPulse, an open-source tool designed to fill that gap by providing CPU and Memory usage metrics for Wasm in a Prometheus-friendly format.  Stop guessing your resource usage, and see what insights WasmPulse can provide to  become the “cAdvisor for WebAssembly” in your monitoring stack!We'll also show Wasm's continuous improvement by providing highlights from our IEEE CCNC 2026 paper, comparing bare-metal processes, Containers, and Wasm (AOT/JIT) on key metrics, such as: artifact size, startup time, E2E network latency and CPU/Memory usage. The results position Wasm as an efficient, performant and sustainable foundation for next-generation cloud-native platforms.
